## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Write the recurrence relation for finding the minimum and maximum of an array using the divide and conquer method. What is the total number of comparisons performed?*

**Model Answer:**

The recurrence relation for the divide and conquer min-max algorithm is:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} 0, & n = 1 \\ 1, & n = 2 \\ 2T(n/2) + 2, & n > 2 \end{cases}$$

Here, the term $2T(n/2)$ represents the recursive calls on the two halves, and the constant $+2$ accounts for the 2 comparisons made in the combine phase (one to find the global minimum, one to find the global maximum).

Solving this recurrence using the Master Theorem (with $a=2, b=2, f(n)=2$) yields the closed-form solution:

$$T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2 \text{ comparisons}$$

This is asymptotically $\Theta(n)$, which is superior to the naive method's $2n - 2$ comparisons, representing a 25% reduction in the constant factor for large $n$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*State the base cases of the divide and conquer min-max algorithm. Why is the n=2 base case important for achieving optimality?*

**Model Answer:**

The divide and conquer min-max algorithm uses two base cases:

**Base Case 1: Single Element ($n = 1$)**
- Condition: `low == high`
- Action: Return the single element as both minimum and maximum.
- Comparisons: **0**

**Base Case 2: Two Elements ($n = 2$)**
- Condition: `high == low + 1`
- Action: Compare the two elements once; assign the smaller to `min` and the larger to `max`.
- Comparisons: **1**

**Importance of the $n = 2$ base case:**

If we recurse all the way down to $n = 1$ base case only, the recurrence becomes $T(n) = 2T(n/1) + 2 \cdot \log_2 n$, which after expansion gives $2n - 2$ comparisons — the same as the naive method. By handling $n = 2$ directly with just **1 comparison** to extract both the min and max simultaneously, we save one comparison at every pair level. This optimization at the leaves is precisely what brings the total down to $3n/2 - 2$.

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the divide and conquer approach to find the minimum and maximum in an array. Write the algorithm and derive the recurrence relation. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the input array `[5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3, 7, 4]`, trace the algorithm step-by-step showing all recursive calls and the comparisons made. Also state the total number of comparisons. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The divide and conquer approach works in three phases:

**Phase 1 — DIVIDE:** Given an array `A[low..high]`, compute the mid-point:

$$mid = \lfloor (low + high) / 2 \rfloor$$

This splits the problem into two sub-arrays of roughly equal size: `A[low..mid]` and `A[mid+1..high]`.

**Phase 2 — CONQUER:** Recursively invoke the algorithm on both sub-arrays until the base cases are reached.

- **Base Case 1:** If `low == high` (single element), return `(A[low], A[low])`.
- **Base Case 2:** If `high == low + 1` (two elements), perform one comparison and return `(min, max)`.

**Phase 3 — COMBINE:** Given `(minL, maxL)` from the left half and `(minR, maxR)` from the right half, perform exactly **2 comparisons**:

$$globalMin = \min(minL, minR)$$
$$globalMax = \max(maxL, maxR)$$

**Recurrence Relation Derivation:**

Let $T(n)$ be the number of comparisons for an array of size $n$.

- For $n = 1$: $T(1) = 0$ (no comparison needed).
- For $n = 2$: $T(2) = 1$ (one comparison in base case).
- For $n > 2$: Two recursive calls on sub-problems of size $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ and $\lceil n/2 \rceil$, plus 2 comparisons in the combine phase:

$$T(n) = T(\lfloor n/2 \rfloor) + T(\lceil n/2 \rceil) + 2$$

Assuming $n = 2^k$ for simplicity:

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$$

Solving by the recursion tree method or Master Theorem gives:

$$T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2$$

**Model Solution for (b) — Step-by-Step Trace for A = [5, 2, 8, 1, 9, 3, 7, 4]:**

| Recursive Call | Sub-array | Action | Result | Cmps |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `findMinMax(0,7)` | `[5,2,8,1,9,3,7,4]` | mid=3, recurse | — | — |
| `findMinMax(0,3)` | `[5,2,8,1]` | mid=1, recurse | — | — |
| `findMinMax(0,1)` | `[5,2]` | 5<2? No → min=2, max=5 | (2, 5) | 1 |
| `findMinMax(2,3)` | `[8,1]` | 8<1? No → min=1, max=8 | (1, 8) | 1 |
| **Combine** (0,3) | — | min(2,1)=1; max(5,8)=8 | (1, 8) | 2 |
| `findMinMax(4,7)` | `[9,3,7,4]` | mid=5, recurse | — | — |
| `findMinMax(4,5)` | `[9,3]` | 9<3? No → min=3, max=9 | (3, 9) | 1 |
| `findMinMax(6,7)` | `[7,4]` | 7<4? No → min=4, max=7 | (4, 7) | 1 |
| **Combine** (4,7) | — | min(3,4)=3; max(9,7)=9 | (3, 9) | 2 |
| **Combine** (0,7) | — | min(1,3)=1; max(8,9)=9 | (1, 9) | 2 |

**Total comparisons = 1 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 10**

**Verification:** $3n/2 - 2 = 3(8)/2 - 2 = 10$ ✓

**Final Answer:** Minimum = 1, Maximum = 9.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Compare the divide and conquer approach with the simple/min-max method for finding the minimum and maximum of an array. Show that the divide and conquer method is asymptotically optimal. (7 marks)

**(b)** Implement the divide and conquer min-max algorithm in C. Show the output for the input array `[12, 3, 5, 7, 19, 1, 8, 11]`. Compute the time complexity. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Simple Method | Divide and Conquer |
|--------|--------------|--------------------|
| **Approach** | Sequential scan twice | Recursive splitting |
| **Comparisons** | $2n - 2$ | $3n/2 - 2$ |
| **Asymptotic Class** | $\Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| **Constant Factor** | 2 | 1.5 (better by 25%) |
| **Space** | $O(1)$ iterative | $O(\log n)$ recursion stack |
| **Parallelizable?** | No | Yes (natural parallelism) |
| **Implementation** | Simple loop | Recursive with base cases |

**Asymptotic Optimality Proof:**

The lower bound for any comparison-based algorithm to find both min and max is $\lceil 3n/2 \rceil - 2$ (proven by information-theoretic arguments — we need to distinguish the maximum from the minimum in a set of $n$ elements).

Since the divide and conquer algorithm achieves $3n/2 - 2$ comparisons, which **matches this lower bound**, the algorithm is **asymptotically and exactly optimal** in the comparison model.

By the Master Theorem with $a=2, b=2, f(n)=2$:

$$n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = n, \quad f(n) = 2 = O(n^{0.99})$$

Thus $T(n) = \Theta(n)$, which is the best possible for a linear scan problem.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**C Program:**

```c
#include <stdio.h>

struct Pair {
    int min;
    int max;
};

struct Pair findMinMax(int arr[], int low, int high) {
    struct Pair res, left, right;
    int mid;

    if (low == high) {
        res.min = arr[low];
        res.max = arr[low];
        return res;
    }

    if (high == low + 1) {
        if (arr[low] < arr[high]) {
            res.min = arr[low];
            res.max = arr[high];
        } else {
            res.min = arr[high];
            res.max = arr[low];
        }
        return res;
    }

    mid = low + (high - low) / 2;
    left  = findMinMax(arr, low, mid);
    right = findMinMax(arr, mid + 1, high);

    res.min = (left.min < right.min) ? left.min : right.min;
    res.max = (left.max > right.max) ? left.max : right.max;

    return res;
}

int main() {
    int arr[] = {12, 3, 5, 7, 19, 1, 8, 11};
    int n = sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[0]);
    struct Pair ans = findMinMax(arr, 0, n - 1);

    printf("Input Array: 12 3 5 7 19 1 8 11\n");
    printf("Minimum element = %d\n", ans.min);
    printf("Maximum element = %d\n", ans.max);

    return 0;
}
```

**Output:**

```
Input Array: 12 3 5 7 19 1 8 11
Minimum element = 1
Maximum element = 19
```

**Time Complexity Computation:**

Let $T(n)$ be the number of comparisons.

$$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$$

Apply Master Theorem: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 2$.

$$n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$$

Since $f(n) = O(n^{1 - \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 0.5$, we are in **Case 1**:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$$

Therefore, the time complexity is $O(n)$ in the best, average, and worst cases.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the n=2 base case** — Recursing all the way to n=1 yields $2n-2$ comparisons (same as naive). Marks deducted: **3 marks**.
> 2. **Off-by-one in mid calculation** — Using `mid = (low + high) / 2` is fine, but using `(low + high + 1) / 2` or omitting `+1` for the right half boundary causes infinite recursion or wrong answers. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Combine step using more than 2 comparisons** — Adding unnecessary comparisons (e.g., comparing `minL` with `maxR`) breaks the $3n/2 - 2$ optimality. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing the recurrence formula** — Writing $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1)$ without specifying the constant 2 is imprecise for this specific algorithm. Marks deducted: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Not showing the closed-form** — Students often write the recurrence but skip the derivation to $3n/2 - 2$. The closed form is essential to prove optimality. Marks deducted: **2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme Module Experiment 2**
>
> - **Paradigm:** Divide and Conquer has 3 strict phases — **Divide, Conquer, Combine**.
> - **Problem:** Given an unsorted array, find both min and max in minimum comparisons.
> - **Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$ with $T(1)=0$ and $T(2)=1$.
> - **Closed-form:** $T(n) = 3n/2 - 2$ for $n$ a power of 2; $3\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ in general.
> - **Asymptotic:** $\Theta(n)$ — linear time, matches the optimal lower bound.
> - **Combine cost:** Exactly **2 comparisons** (one for min, one for max). Never more.
> - **Base cases:** Two of them — `n=1` (0 cmps) and `n=2` (1 cmp). The `n=2` case is what makes the algorithm optimal.
> - **Time complexity:** Best = Average = Worst = $O(n)$.
> - **Space complexity:** $O(\log n)$ for the recursion stack; can be reduced to $O(1)$ iteratively.
> - **Master Theorem:** $a=2$, $b=2$, $f(n)=2$, falls in **Case 1** → $\Theta(n)$.
> - **Edge cases to test in lab:** Empty array (undefined behavior — handle separately), 1-element array, 2-element array, all-equal elements, sorted and reverse-sorted arrays.
> - **Code structure:** Use a `struct Pair {int min, int max;}` to return both values from each recursive call.
> - **Naive vs D&C savings:** D&C saves **25%** of comparisons asymptotically ($2n-2$ vs $3n/2 - 2$).
> - **Common KTU exam trap:** Students often write $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1)$ — the constant **must be 2**, not just $O(1)$.
> - **Real-world usage:** Sensor data filtering, database range queries, image processing pipelines, statistical range computation in ML preprocessing.
> - **Alternative approaches:** Tournament method (same $3n/2-2$ cost), Pairwise comparison method (process in pairs, then compare candidates — also $3\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$).

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Merge Sort** is a comparison-based, **divide-and-conquer** sorting algorithm that recursively divides an input array into two nearly equal halves, sorts each half independently, and then **merges** the two sorted halves to produce a single fully sorted output. It is a **stable**, **non-in-place** (out-of-place) sorting procedure that guarantees a worst-case time complexity of **O(n log n)** for any input distribution.

According to the KTU 2024 Algorithm Analysis and Design Lab syllabus (PCITL508), Merge Sort is treated as the canonical pedagogical example of the *divide and conquer* paradigm because it cleanly separates the three stages of the strategy: **Divide → Conquer (Recurse) → Combine (Merge)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCITL508 – Module 2):**
> Merge Sort is the prescribed experiment to demonstrate the divide-and-conquer paradigm, the merge subroutine, and recurrence relation analysis using the **Master Theorem**. Students are expected to implement, trace, and analyze the algorithm — not merely memorize its steps.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have **two neatly stacked, pre-sorted piles of playing cards** in your left and right hand. You want to build one final sorted pile on the table. You repeatedly compare the **top (smallest face-up) card** of each pile, pick the smaller one, and place it on the output pile. The act of repeatedly choosing the smaller exposed top card is the **MERGE** operation.

But how do you *get* the two sorted piles in the first place?

You **divide** the original messy deck into two halves. If a half still contains more than one card, you recursively apply the same rule: split that half again. Eventually, every sub-pile contains either one card (trivially sorted) or is empty. Then you walk back up the recursion and **merge** pairs of sorted piles. This walk-back-up-and-merge is the heart of Merge Sort.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for students:** Merge Sort does **not** sort *within* a sub-array directly. It relies on the fact that a single element is always sorted. All the "real work" happens during the **MERGE** step, not during the division.

### Boundary Conditions & Standard Metrics

- **Time Complexity (Worst / Average / Best):** **O(n log n)** in **all three cases**.
- **Space Complexity:** **O(n)** auxiliary space for the temporary left/right arrays.
- **Stability:** **Stable** — equal-keyed elements retain their original relative order.
- **In-place:** **No** (requires additional memory proportional to n).
- **Recurrence Relation:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$.
- **Sentinel Value Used in Textbook MERGE:** $+\infty$ (or any value ≥ all keys).

---

## Why Merge Sort Matters in Engineering

Merge Sort is the workhorse behind several production-grade systems:

- **External Sorting (Databases):** When data exceeds RAM, the merge step naturally extends to disk-resident runs.
- **Standard Library Implementations:** Python's `Timsort`, Java's `Arrays.sort()` for objects, and Perl's internal sort all use merge-sort-derived strategies.
- **Linked List Sorting:** Achieves **O(n log n)** time with **O(1)** extra space (no random access needed).
- **Inversion Counting:** Modified merge step can count inversions in O(n log n).
- **Parallel Computing:** The two recursive halves are independent — perfectly suited for fork/join parallelism.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Divide-and-Conquer Anatomy

Merge Sort strictly obeys the three-stage **divide-and-conquer** template. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU lab viva questions.

### Stage 1 — DIVIDE
The problem of sorting sub-array $A[p \ldots r]$ is divided into two sub-problems:
- Sort $A[p \ldots q]$
- Sort $A[q+1 \ldots r]$

where $q = \lfloor (p+r)/2 \rfloor$.

### Stage 2 — CONQUER (Recurse)
Sort the two sub-arrays **recursively** using merge sort itself. The base case of the recursion is when a sub-array has **at most one element** ($p \geq r$), which is trivially sorted.

### Stage 3 — COMBINE (Merge)
Merge the two sorted sub-arrays $A[p \ldots q]$ and $A[q+1 \ldots r]$ to produce a single sorted sub-array $A[p \ldots r]$. This is the only stage that does non-trivial work.

---

## 2.2 The MERGE Subroutine (The Core Engine)

The MERGE procedure runs in **linear time** $\Theta(n)$ where $n = r - p + 1$.

**Inputs:** Array $A$, indices $p$, $q$, $r$ such that $p \leq q < r$ and both $A[p \ldots q]$ and $A[q+1 \ldots r]$ are individually sorted.

**Strategy:**
1. Copy the left half into temporary array $L[1 \ldots n_1]$ where $n_1 = q - p + 1$.
2. Copy the right half into temporary array $R[1 \ldots n_2]$ where $n_2 = r - q$.
3. Append a **sentinel** ($\infty$) at the end of both $L$ and $R$ to avoid boundary checks.
4. Maintain pointers $i = 1$, $j = 1$. For $k = p$ to $r$, repeatedly assign $A[k] = \min(L[i], R[j])$ and advance the corresponding pointer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the sentinel works:** When one array is exhausted, its remaining entries are all $\infty$, so the comparison always picks from the other array. This eliminates the need for explicit `if (i > n1)` boundary tests inside the merge loop.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property** | **Value / Formula** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Recurrence Relation | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | Valid for $n > 1$ |
| Base Case | $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ | Single element is sorted |
| Best-Case Time | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | No early exit in merge step |
| Worst-Case Time | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Independent of input order |
| Average-Case Time | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Independent of input order |
| Auxiliary Space | $\Theta(n)$ | Two temporary arrays of size $n/2$ |
| In-place? | **No** | Requires extra memory |
| Stable? | **Yes** | Equal keys retain input order |
| Number of Comparisons (Worst) | $n \log_2 n - n + 1$ | Approximately |
| Master Theorem Case | Case 2 | $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$ |
| Recursion Tree Levels | $\log_2 n$ | Height of the recursion tree |
| Work per Level | $\Theta(n)$ | Total merge work per level |
| Suitable for Linked Lists | **Yes** | No random access required |
| Suitable for External Sort | **Yes** | Sequential access pattern |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem Application (KTU Favorite Question):**
> For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$:
> Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n$.
> Since $f(n) = \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, this is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, giving $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **External Sorting in Databases:** When a 100 GB table needs sorting but only 4 GB of RAM is available, the DB engine sorts chunks (runs) in memory using something like quick-sort, then performs a **k-way merge** across disk-resident sorted runs. The merge step is exactly the MERGE subroutine extended to $k$ inputs.
- **Sorting Linked Lists in O(1) extra space:** Because merge sort only needs sequential traversal, linked lists can be split and merged without extra storage.
- **Inversion Counting in O(n log n):** Modify the merge step to count how many times an element from the right half is placed before an element from the left half.
- **Parallel Architectures:** Each recursive call is embarrassingly parallel — modern multi-core frameworks (ForkJoinPool, Cilk, TBB) exploit this to achieve near-linear speedup.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The MERGE Procedure — Exhaustive Pseudocode with Commentary

The textbook MERGE procedure (Cormen et al.) is reproduced below with exhaustive explanation of every step. **Each line is annotated** for KTU lab record purposes.

```
MERGE(A, p, q, r)
1   n1 ← q − p + 1                          // size of left sub-array
2   n2 ← r − q                              // size of right sub-array
3   let L[1 .. n1 + 1] and R[1 .. n2 + 1]   // allocate temporary arrays (+1 for sentinel)
4   for i ← 1 to n1                         // copy A[p..q] into L
5       L[i] ← A[p + i − 1]
6   for j ← 1 to n2                         // copy A[q+1..r] into R
7       R[j] ← A[q + j]
8   L[n1 + 1] ← +∞                          // sentinel: any value ≥ all keys
9   R[n2 + 1] ← +∞
10  i ← 1                                   // pointer into L
11  j ← 1                                   // pointer into R
12  for k ← p to r                          // main merge loop
13      if L[i] ≤ R[j]
14          A[k] ← L[i]
15          i ← i + 1
16      else
17          A[k] ← R[j]
18          j ← j + 1
```

### Line-by-Line Logic Explanation

- **Line 1–2:** Compute the lengths of the two halves. For example, if $p=1$, $q=4$, $r=7$, then $n_1 = 4$ and $n_2 = 3$.
- **Line 3:** Allocate two auxiliary arrays of size one greater than the halves to accommodate the sentinel.
- **Line 4–5:** Copy the left half. The offset `p + i − 1` correctly maps `L[1]` to `A[p]`, `L[2]` to `A[p+1]`, etc.
- **Line 6–7:** Copy the right half. `R[1]` maps to `A[q+1]`, `R[2]` to `A[q+2]`, etc.
- **Line 8–9:** SENTINEL insertion — this is the elegance of the algorithm. By placing $+\infty$ at the end of both arrays, the comparison in line 13 *cannot* run off the end without picking the non-sentinel value first.
- **Line 10–11:** Initialize pointers at the start of each temp array.
- **Line 12–18:** The main merge loop runs exactly $r - p + 1$ times (the total number of elements being merged). Each iteration advances exactly one pointer — either $i$ or $j$ — so the loop terminates with $i = n_1 + 1$ and $j = n_2 + 1$.

---

## 3.2 The Complete MERGE-SORT Procedure

```
MERGE-SORT(A, p, r)
1   if p < r
2       q ← ⌊(p + r) / 2⌋
3       MERGE-SORT(A, p, q)         // recursively sort left half
4       MERGE-SORT(A, q + 1, r)     // recursively sort right half
5       MERGE(A, p, q, r)           // merge the two sorted halves
```

The initial call is `MERGE-SORT(A, 1, A.length)` (or `MERGE-SORT(A, 0, n-1)` in 0-indexed languages).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why `if p < r` and not `if p ≤ r`?** Because when $p = r$, the sub-array has exactly **one element**, which is trivially sorted. The recursion base case is the one-element array.

---

## 3.3 Recurrence Relation Derivation

The running time $T(n)$ for merge sort on $n$ elements can be decomposed as:

$$
T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n \leq 1 \\ 2\,T(n/2) + \Theta(n) & \text{if } n > 1 \end{cases}
$$

The three terms are:

- **$2\,T(n/2)$** — the two recursive calls on halves of size $n/2$.
- **$\Theta(n)$** — the merge step, which examines each of the $n$ elements a constant number of times.

### Solving via Recursion Tree

A recursion tree of height $\log_2 n$ has $n$ leaves (each representing a single element). At each level $\ell$ (where $0 \leq \ell \leq \log_2 n$):

- **Number of sub-problems:** $2^\ell$
- **Size of each sub-problem:** $n / 2^\ell$
- **Total work at level $\ell$:** $2^\ell \cdot \Theta(n / 2^\ell) = \Theta(n)$

Since there are $\log_2 n + 1$ levels and each contributes $\Theta(n)$ work, the total work is:

$$
T(n) = \sum_{\ell=0}^{\log_2 n} \Theta(n) = \Theta(n) \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### Master Theorem Verification

For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$:

$$
n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^1 = n
$$

Since $f(n) = \Theta(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, this is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, yielding:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Trace Example (KTU Exam Favorite)

**Input Array:** $A = [5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3, 2, 6]$ (8 elements, 0-indexed: indices 0–7)

**Call:** `MERGE-SORT(A, 0, 7)`

**Recursion tree (splitting phase):**

```
[5, 2, 4, 6, 1, 3, 2, 6]
        ↓ split at q=3
[5, 2, 4, 6]         [1, 3, 2, 6]
   ↓ q=1                ↓ q=5
[5,2]  [4,6]        [1,3]  [2,6]
 ↓      ↓             ↓      ↓
[5][2] [4][6]      [1][3] [2][6]
```

**Merge phase (bottom-up):**

| Step | Merge Operation | Resulting Sub-array |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Merge $[5]$ and $[2]$ | $[2, 5]$ |
| 2 | Merge $[4]$ and $[6]$ | $[4, 6]$ |
| 3 | Merge $[2,5]$ and $[4,6]$ | $[2, 4, 5, 6]$ |
| 4 | Merge $[1]$ and $[3]$ | $[1, 3]$ |
| 5 | Merge $[2]$ and $[6]$ | $[2, 6]$ |
| 6 | Merge $[1,3]$ and $[2,6]$ | $[1, 2, 3, 6]$ |
| 7 | Merge $[2,4,5,6]$ and $[1,2,3,6]$ | $[1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 6]$ |

**Final sorted array:** $[1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 6]$ ✓

---

## 3.5 Complete C Implementation (Lab-Ready)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

void merge(int A[], int p, int q, int r) {
    int n1 = q - p + 1;
    int n2 = r - q;

    int *L = (int *)malloc((n1 + 1) * sizeof(int));
    int *R = (int *)malloc((n2 + 1) * sizeof(int));

    for (int i = 0; i < n1; i++)
        L[i] = A[p + i];
    for (int j = 0; j < n2; j++)
        R[j] = A[q + 1 + j];

    L[n1] = 99999;   // sentinel (assuming keys < 99999)
    R[n2] = 99999;

    int i = 0, j = 0;
    for (int k = p; k <= r; k++) {
        if (L[i] <= R[j]) {
            A[k] = L[i];
            i++;
        } else {
            A[k] = R[j];
            j++;
        }
    }

    free(L);
    free(R);
}

void mergeSort(int A[], int p, int r) {
    if (p < r) {
        int q = (p + r) / 2;
        mergeSort(A, p, q);
        mergeSort(A, q + 1, r);
        merge(A, p, q, r);
    }
}

int main() {
    int n;
    printf("Enter number of elements: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    int *A = (int *)malloc(n * sizeof(int));
    printf("Enter %d elements: ", n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        scanf("%d", &A[i]);

    mergeSort(A, 0, n - 1);

    printf("Sorted array: ");
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        printf("%d ", A[i]);
    printf("\n");

    free(A);
    return 0;
}
```

### Sample Run

```
Enter number of elements: 8
Enter 8 elements: 5 2 4 6 1 3 2 6
Sorted array: 1 2 2 3 4 5 6 6
```

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level Functional Architecture of Merge Sort

The following diagram captures the **recursion + merge** interaction at the architectural level — ideal for a KTU lab record diagram and a viva explanation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT ARRAY A of size n] --> B{p less than r}
    B -- NO --> C[RETURN base case single element sorted]
    B -- YES --> D[COMPUTE q equals floor of p plus r over 2]
    D --> E[RECURSIVE CALL mergeSort on A p to q left half]
    D --> F[RECURSIVE CALL mergeSort on A q plus 1 to r right half]
    E --> G[CALL MERGE on A p q r]
    F --> G
    G --> H[OUTPUT merged sorted subarray A p to r]
    H --> I[RETURN to parent call]
    C --> I
    I --> J[FINAL SORTED ARRAY]
```

---

## 4.2 The MERGE Subroutine — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[START MERGE A p q r] --> M2[COMPUTE n1 equals q minus p plus 1]
    M2 --> M3[COMPUTE n2 equals r minus q]
    M3 --> M4[ALLOCATE temp array L of size n1 plus 1]
    M4 --> M5[ALLOCATE temp array R of size n2 plus 1]
    M5 --> M6[COPY A p to q into L 1 to n1]
    M6 --> M7[COPY A q plus 1 to r into R 1 to n2]
    M7 --> M8[SET L n1 plus 1 equals INFINITY sentinel]
    M8 --> M9[SET R n2 plus 1 equals INFINITY sentinel]
    M9 --> M10[INITIALIZE i equals 1 and j equals 1]
    M10 --> M11[LOOP k from p to r]
    M11 --> M12{L i less than or equal to R j}
    M12 -- YES --> M13[SET A k equals L i then i plus 1]
    M12 -- NO --> M14[SET A k equals R j then j plus 1]
    M13 --> M15{k equals r}
    M14 --> M15
    M15 -- NO --> M11
    M15 -- YES --> M16[FREE temp arrays L and R]
    M16 --> M17[END MERGE]
```

---

```mermaid
    N0["A 0 7 size 8"] --> N1["A 0 3 size 4"]

    N8 --> M1
    M1 --> M2["MERGE 0 1 3 yields 2 4 5 6"]
    N9 --> M2
    N10 --> M2
    M2 --> M3["MERGE 0 3 7 yields 1 2 2 3 4 5 6 6"]

```
