# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart of Kruskal's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> input[/Read graph G = V, E, w/]
    input --> initDSU[Initialise DSU: MAKE-SET v for all v in V]
    initDSU --> sortE[Sort all edges by weight ascending]
    sortE --> mstInit[MST = empty set, totalWeight = 0]
    mstInit --> loopStart{For each edge u, v, w in sorted order}
    loopStart -->|no more edges| checkDone
    loopStart -->|next edge| findCheck{FIND u != FIND v?}
    findCheck -->|No cycle| addEdge[Add u, v to MST]
    addEdge --> unionOp[UNION u, v]
    unionOp --> weightAdd[totalWeight = totalWeight + w]
    weightAdd --> sizeCheck{|MST| = n - 1?}
    sizeCheck -->|Yes| outputResult
    sizeCheck -->|No| loopStart
    findCheck -->|Cycle| reject[Reject edge u, v]
    reject --> loopStart
    checkDone{|MST| = n - 1?}
    checkDone -->|Yes| outputResult[/Print MST edges and total weight/]
    checkDone -->|No| forestWarn[/Output Minimum Spanning Forest, graph disconnected/]
    outputResult --> stop([End])
    forestWarn --> stop
```

## 4.2 DSU Operations — Find and Union

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Vertex u] --> findU{FIND u}
    findU --> pathComp[Path compression: point u to root]
    pathComp --> rootU((Root ru))
    nodeB[Vertex v] --> findV{FIND v}
    findV --> rootV((Root rv))
    rootU --> sameCheck{ru == rv?}
    sameCheck -->|Yes| noOp[Already in same set, do nothing]
    sameCheck -->|No| rankCheck{rank ru >= rank rv?}
    rankCheck -->|Yes| attach1[Attach rv under ru]
    rankCheck -->|No| attach2[Attach ru under rv]
    attach1 --> merged((Merged set))
    attach2 --> merged
```

## 4.3 Original Graph G (5 vertices, 7 edges)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeE((E))
    nodeA ---|4| nodeB
    nodeA ---|1| nodeC
    nodeB ---|2| nodeC
    nodeB ---|5| nodeD
    nodeC ---|8| nodeD
    nodeC ---|10| nodeE
    nodeD ---|2| nodeE
```

## 4.4 Resulting Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    nodeD((D))
    nodeE((E))
    nodeA ---|1| nodeC
    nodeB ---|2| nodeC
    nodeB ---|5| nodeD
    nodeD ---|2| nodeE
```

**Total MST Weight = 1 + 2 + 5 + 2 = 10**

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputLayer[Input Layer]
        G[Graph G: V, E, w]
    end
    subgraph preprocessing[Preprocessing Module]
        initDSU[DSU Initialiser]
        sortModule[Edge Sorter O m log m]
    end
    subgraph greedyCore[Greedy Core]
        edgeSelector[Minimum-Weight Edge Selector]
        cycleChecker[Cycle Detector via DSU FIND]
        unionOp[DSU UNION]
        mstBuilder[MST Edge Accumulator]
    end
    subgraph outputLayer[Output Layer]
        result[MST Edge List + Total Weight]
    end
    G --> initDSU
    G --> sortModule
    sortModule --> edgeSelector
    initDSU --> cycleChecker
    edgeSelector --> cycleChecker
    cycleChecker -->|no cycle| unionOp
    cycleChecker -->|cycle| edgeSelector
    unionOp --> mstBuilder
    mstBuilder --> edgeSelector
    mstBuilder --> result
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**State and explain the Cut Property and Cycle Property of minimum spanning trees. How does Kruskal's algorithm use them?**

**Model Answer:**

**Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of a connected graph $G$, the **lightest edge** crossing the cut belongs to *every* MST of $G$. *Proof sketch:* If the lightest edge $e$ is not in some MST $T$, adding $e$ to $T$ creates a cycle. That cycle must contain another edge $e'$ crossing the same cut, and $w(e) \leq w(e')$. Swapping $e'$ with $e$ yields a spanning tree no heavier than $T$, so $T$ cannot be minimum unless it already contains $e$.

**Cycle Property:** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the **heaviest edge** in $C$ is *not* in any MST. *Proof sketch:* If the heaviest edge $e$ of $C$ were in MST $T$, removing $e$ disconnects $T$ into two components. Some other edge $e'$ of $C$ reconnects them, and $w(e') \leq w(e)$. Replacing $e$ by $e'$ yields a strictly lighter or equal spanning tree.

**Use in Kruskal's:** At every step, the edges already in MST partition $V$ into components. The "next edge" considered is the minimum-weight edge connecting two *different* components — exactly a light edge crossing the cut defined by these components. The Cut Property guarantees its safety. Equivalently, rejected edges would form a cycle with existing edges; by the Cycle Property, such heavy edges are unsafe.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for stating the two properties + 1 mark for the Kruskal's connection.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]
**Why is Kruskal's algorithm called a "greedy" algorithm? What is the role of the Disjoint Set Union (DSU) data structure in it?**

**Model Answer:**

Kruskal's algorithm is termed *greedy* because at **every local decision step** it picks the **locally optimal** choice — the smallest-weight edge that does not create a cycle — with the hope (here, the **guarantee**, by the Cut Property) that this locally optimal choice is also **globally optimal** for the entire MST.

**Role of DSU:**
1. **Cycle Detection in (near) constant time** — When considering an edge $(u, v)$, Kruskal must check whether $u$ and $v$ are already in the same connected component of the partial forest. This check is implemented as `FIND(u) != FIND(v)`, which runs in $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized time.
2. **Component Merging** — When the edge is accepted, `UNION(u, v)` merges the two components to maintain the invariant.

Without DSU, a naive cycle check would cost $O(n)$ via DFS, leading to an overall complexity of $O(m \cdot n)$ — strictly worse than the $O(m \log m)$ DSU-based approach.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for greedy justification + 1 mark for DSU cycle detection + 1 mark for DSU complexity advantage.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Apply Kruskal's algorithm to find the MST of the following graph. Show all steps clearly and compute the total MST weight. **(7 marks)**

**Edges:** (A, B, 7), (A, C, 8), (B, C, 3), (B, D, 6), (C, D, 4), (C, E, 2), (D, E, 5).

**(b)** Write the time complexity analysis of Kruskal's algorithm and explain how DSU achieves near-constant time per operation. Compare it briefly with Prim's algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Step 1 — Sort edges by weight (ascending):**

| Order | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | C – E | 2 |
| 2 | B – C | 3 |
| 3 | C – D | 4 |
| 4 | D – E | 5 |
| 5 | B – D | 6 |
| 6 | A – B | 7 |
| 7 | A – C | 8 |

**Step 2 — Initialize DSU:** Each vertex in its own set: $\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}$.

**Step 3 — Process edges in order:**

| # | Edge | w | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Cycle? | Action | Components | MST Edges | Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | C–E | 2 | C | E | No | ACCEPT | {A},{B},{C,E},{D} | 1 | 2 |
| 2 | B–C | 3 | B | C | No | ACCEPT | {A},{B,C,E},{D} | 2 | 5 |
| 3 | C–D | 4 | B | D | No | ACCEPT | {A},{B,C,D,E} | 3 | 9 |
| 4 | D–E | 5 | B | B | Yes | REJECT | {A},{B,C,D,E} | 3 | 9 |
| 5 | B–D | 6 | B | B | Yes | REJECT | {A},{B,C,D,E} | 3 | 9 |
| 6 | A–B | 7 | A | B | No | ACCEPT | {A,B,C,D,E} | 4 | 16 |
| 7 | A–C | 8 | — | — | — | STOP (n−1=4 reached) | — | 4 | 16 |

**Final MST:** $\{(C, E, 2), (B, C, 3), (C, D, 4), (A, B, 7)\}$

$$
\boxed{W(\text{MST}) = 2 + 3 + 4 + 7 = 16}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

The algorithm has three main phases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{MAKE-SET}} &= O(n) \\
T_{\text{sort}} &= O(m \log m) \\
T_{\text{process edges}} &= m \cdot O(\alpha(n)) \approx O(m) \\
T_{\text{total}} &= O(m \log m)
\end{aligned}
$$

Sorting dominates, so **Kruskal's overall time complexity is $O(m \log m)$**, or equivalently $O(E \log E)$. Space complexity is $O(n + m)$.

**How DSU Achieves Near-Constant Time:**

The DSU data structure supports two operations — `FIND(x)` and `UNION(x, y)` — using two key optimizations:

1. **Path Compression** in `FIND`: every node on the path to the root is re-parented directly to the root, flattening the tree. Amortized cost: $O(\alpha(n))$.
2. **Union by Rank**: when merging two trees, the root of the *shorter* tree is attached under the root of the *taller* tree. This keeps the tree height logarithmic at worst, and the combination of both optimizations yields the inverse Ackermann function $\alpha(n)$.

The function $\alpha(n)$ grows *so slowly* that for all $n$ up to $10^{80}$ (more than the number of atoms in the observable universe), $\alpha(n) < 5$. Hence DSU operations are effectively $O(1)$ in practice.

**Comparison with Prim's Algorithm:**

| Aspect | Kruskal's | Prim's |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Edge-based (process lightest edge globally) | Vertex-based (grow one tree) |
| Data Structure | DSU + sorted edge list | Min-priority queue (min-heap) |
| Time (Binary Heap) | $O(m \log m)$ | $O((n + m) \log n)$ |
| Time (Fibonacci Heap) | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(n \log n + m)$ |
| Better for | **Sparse graphs** ($m \approx n$) | **Dense graphs** ($m \approx n^2$) |
| Cycle detection | Explicit (DSU FIND) | Implicit (only adjacent edges considered) |
| Implementation complexity | Moderate (DSU) | Moderate (heap with Decrease-Key) |

For the KTU lab record, both are equally valid; choice depends on graph density.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** For the graph with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ and edge list — $(1, 2, 3), (1, 3, 1), (2, 3, 4), (2, 4, 2), (3, 4, 5), (3, 5, 6), (4, 5, 7)$ — apply Kruskal's algorithm to obtain the MST. Show the DSU state and MST weight at each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "Kruskal's algorithm fails on a disconnected graph." Discuss this statement. How would you modify the algorithm to handle disconnected input? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Step 1 — Sort edges:**

| Order | Edge | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 – 3 | 1 |
| 2 | 2 – 4 | 2 |
| 3 | 1 – 2 | 3 |
| 4 | 2 – 3 | 4 |
| 5 | 3 – 4 | 5 |
| 6 | 3 – 5 | 6 |
| 7 | 4 – 5 | 7 |

**Step 2 — DSU trace:**

| # | Edge | w | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Cycle? | Action | Components | MST Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1–3 | 1 | 1 | 3 | No | ACCEPT | {1,3},{2},{4},{5} | 1 |
| 2 | 2–4 | 2 | 2 | 4 | No | ACCEPT | {1,3},{2,4},{5} | 3 |
| 3 | 1–2 | 3 | 1 | 2 | No | ACCEPT | {1,2,3,4},{5} | 6 |
| 4 | 2–3 | 4 | 1 | 1 | Yes | REJECT | {1,2,3,4},{5} | 6 |
| 5 | 3–4 | 5 | 1 | 1 | Yes | REJECT | {1,2,3,4},{5} | 6 |
| 6 | 3–5 | 6 | 1 | 5 | No | ACCEPT | {1,2,3,4,5} | 12 |

**Final MST:** $\{(1, 3, 1), (2, 4, 2), (1, 2, 3), (3, 5, 6)\}$

$$
\boxed{W(\text{MST}) = 1 + 2 + 3 + 6 = 12}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**Discussion of the Statement:**

The statement is **partially incorrect** — Kruskal's algorithm does not "fail" on a disconnected graph in the sense of producing an erroneous result. Rather, it naturally **terminates with $|MST| < n - 1$**, and the result is a **Minimum Spanning Forest (MSF)** — a collection of minimum spanning trees, one for each connected component of the input graph.

For example, consider a graph with two components: $\{A, B\}$ connected by a single edge, and $\{C, D, E\}$ with internal edges. After processing all edges, Kruskal's returns **two disjoint trees**, not one. This is the *mathematically correct* answer to the problem "minimize the total weight of an acyclic subgraph that is as connected as possible."

**Modification for Disconnected Input:**

To make Kruskal's output a spanning tree even on disconnected inputs:

1. **Add a "super-source" node** $s$ connected to every other vertex with an edge of weight **0**. Apply Kruskal's to this augmented graph. The resulting MST of size $n - 1$ contains the original MST plus zero-weight edges from $s$ to each component. Removing $s$ and its incident edges yields a tree on the original vertices.
2. **Algorithmically**: simply remove the cycle-check; the algorithm then degenerates to a topological sort / union that merges vertices greedily, but this sacrifices correctness.
3. **Practical approach**: in the lab, validate connectivity first using a `DFS` or `FIND`-based check; if $|MST| < n - 1$ at termination, print "Graph is disconnected; produced a Minimum Spanning Forest."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to SORT the edges** before iteration. This is the **#1** reason students lose full marks on Kruskal's. Without sorting, you are not running Kruskal's algorithm — you are running a meaningless variant.
> 2. **Confusing MST (n−1 edges) with the full edge list**. Students often list all "accepted" edges but miss that one was rejected as a cycle. Always include the *cycle-rejection row* explicitly in your table.
> 3. **Reporting the wrong MST weight**: an off-by-one edge gives a wrong total. Cross-check: $n - 1$ edges accepted, weight = sum of those weights only.
> 4. **Confusing Kruskal's with Prim's**: Kruskal processes **edges globally**; Prim's grows a **single tree vertex-by-vertex**. A description that "picks the minimum edge incident to the tree" describes Prim's, not Kruskal's.
> 5. **Ignoring disconnected graphs**: failing to state what happens on disconnected input loses you 1–2 marks even on a connected-input question.
> 6. **Omitting DSU**: just saying "check for cycle" without naming DSU / Union-Find loses 1 mark. KTU examiners look for the data structure name.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision? Memorize this block.**

- [x] **MST definition:** Acyclic, connected, spans all $n$ vertices, exactly $n - 1$ edges, minimum total weight.
- [x] **Kruskal's is a GREEDY, edge-based, global-selection algorithm.**
- [x] **Three mandatory steps:** (1) Sort edges by weight, (2) Iterate in sorted order, (3) Accept edge iff it does not create a cycle.
- [x] **Cycle detection = DSU FIND operation.** Two optimizations: **Path Compression + Union by Rank** give $\alpha(n)$ amortized time.
- [x] **Time complexity:** $\boxed{O(m \log m)}$ or equivalently $O(E \log E)$. **Space:** $O(n + m)$.
- [x] **Correctness foundation:** **Cut Property** (light edge across a cut is in every MST) — applied at every selection step.
- [x] **For a connected graph, MST has exactly $n - 1$ edges** — early termination condition.
- [x] **For a disconnected graph:** Kruskal's produces a **Minimum Spanning Forest (MSF)** with $|MST| = n - c$, where $c$ = number of connected components.
- [x] **Kruskal's vs. Prim's:** Kruskal's is better for **sparse** graphs ($m \approx n$); Prim's (with binary heap) is better for **dense** graphs ($m \approx n^2$). With Fibonacci heap, Prim's reaches $O(n \log n + m)$.
- [x] **Real-world use cases:** Telecom cable laying, network design, VLSI routing, power grids, single-linkage clustering, MST-based 2-approximation for TSP.
- [x] **Common lab mistakes to avoid:** Un-sorted iteration, missing the cycle-rejection row, wrong total weight, confusing Kruskal's with Prim's.
- [x] **Memorize the trace format:** a table with columns `Edge | Weight | FIND(u) | FIND(v) | Cycle? | Action | Components | MST Weight` — KTU examiners reward clean, tabular traces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Prim's Algorithm to Find Minimum Spanning Tree
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCITL508 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Implement Prim's Algorithm to Find Minimum Spanning Tree # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST):** Given a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$ where $w: E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}^+$ is the weight function, a **Minimum Spanning Tree** is a spanning subgraph $T = (V, E')$ such that: - $T$ is a tree (acyclic and connected). - $E' \subseteq E$ with $\vert E' \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$. - The total weight $W(T) = \sum_{e \in E'} w(e)$ is **minimum** among all possible spanning trees. **Prim's Algorithm** is a **greedy algorithm** that grows the MST one vertex at a time. At every step, it picks the **minimum weight edge** that connects a vertex already inside the MST to a vertex that is still outside. The algorithm halts when all $\vert V \vert$ vertices have been included, yielding exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges in the resulting spanning tree. > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard):** > - **Cut of a graph:** A partition $(S, V \setminus S)$ of the vertex set. > - **Light edge (across a cut):** The edge with the smallest weight crossing the cut. > - **Cut Property:** A light edge across any cut is safe to add to the MST. > - **Greedy-Choice Property:** A locally optimal (minimum weight) choice leads to a globally optimal MST. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Village Road Builder" Imagine you are a **village engineer** tasked with connecting **5 hamlets** ($A, B, C, D, E$) using **new roads** so that every hamlet is reachable, but the **total road-building cost is minimum**. You are given a quote for every possible road segment. **Your Greedy Rule:** *At each step, lay the cheapest road that connects a hamlet already on your network to a hamlet that is still isolated.* You repeat this until no hamlet is left isolated. **Mapping back to the algorithm:** | Real-World Step | Algorithm Equivalent | | :--- | :--- | | Hamlets already on the road network | Vertices in the growing MST set $S$ | | Cheap road connecting new hamlet | Minimum weight edge $(u, v)$ with $u \in S$, $v \notin S$ | | Cheapest available quote | Edge with minimum key value in priority queue | | Network fully connected | MST complete with $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does greedy work here?** Because every intermediate "network" you build is a **prefix of some MST**. The cut property guarantees that the cheapest edge crossing the boundary is **always safe** — it can never cause a cycle and can never block the optimal solution. ## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Exam-Relevant) The following metrics are universally tested in KTU exams for any MST algorithm: - **Time Complexity (Adjacency Matrix):** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ - **Time Complexity (Binary Heap + Adjacency List):** $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ - **Time Complexity (Fibonacci Heap):** $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ - **Edges in MST:** Exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ (assuming $G$ is connected) - **Total MST Weight:** $W_{MST} = \sum_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert - 1} w(e_i)$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Heuristic:** If the problem says "dense graph", use the $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ matrix variant. If the problem says "sparse graph" or large $E$, use the heap variant. KTU values will often explicitly mark this distinction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Algorithmic Foundation — The Greedy Choice Prim's algorithm is a direct embodiment of the **Generalized Greedy Strategy** for optimization. For it to produce a globally optimal solution, two mathematical properties must hold — both are tested frequently in KTU 2024 scheme exams: ### 2.1.1 Greedy-Choice Property At each iteration, choosing the **minimum-weight edge** $(u, v)$ with $u \in S$ and $v \notin S$ (where $S$ is the current MST vertex set) does **not preclude** the existence of an optimal solution. Formally: $$\exists \text{ MST } T^* \text{ such that } (u, v) \in T^*$$ ### 2.1.2 Optimal Substructure After adding the greedy edge $(u, v)$ to the partial MST, the remaining subproblem — finding the MST of $G' = (V, E, w)$ restricted to vertices $V \setminus \{u\}$ plus the constraint that $v$ is now in $S$ — is **identical in form** to the original problem. ### 2.1.3 Cut Property (Theorem) Let $C = (S, V \setminus S)$ be any cut of $G$. Let $e^*$ be the unique minimum-weight edge crossing $C$ (break ties arbitrarily). Then $e^*$ is contained in **every MST** of $G$. **Proof Sketch (frequently asked as a 7-mark question):** 1. Suppose an MST $T$ does not contain $e^*$. 2. Since $T$ is a spanning tree, there must exist some edge $e \in T$ crossing the same cut $C$. 3. Adding $e^*$ to $T$ creates a cycle; since $e$ also crosses $C$, removing $e$ from this cycle yields another spanning tree $T'$. 4. $W(T') = W(T) + w(e^*) - w(e) \leq W(T)$ because $w(e^*) \leq w(e)$. 5. If $w(e^*) < w(e)$, then $T$ was not minimum — contradiction. If $w(e^*) = w(e)$, then $T' = T \cup \{e^*\} \setminus \{e\}$ is also a minimum spanning tree containing $e^*$. > [!NOTE] > **Cycle Property (Dual):** For any cycle $C$ in $G$, the edge with **maximum** weight in $C$ is **never** in any MST (provided its weight is strictly greater). This is the basis for Kruskal's algorithm. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept / Property | Mathematical Formulation | Domain / Range | Exam Relevance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | MST edge count | $\vert E_{MST} \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$ | $\vert V \vert \geq 2$ | 3-mark direct | | MST total weight | $W_{MST} = \sum_{e \in E'} w(e)$ | $w(e) > 0$ | All questions | | Cut property statement | $\forall \text{ cut } C: \min_{e \in \delta(C)} w(e) \in T_{MST}$ | Weighted graphs | 7-mark derivation | | Time (Matrix) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ | Dense graph | 3-mark compare | | Time (Binary Heap) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Sparse graph | 3-mark compare | | Time (Fibonacci Heap) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | Theoretical optimal | Rare | | Space | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Universal | 3-mark | | Greedy invariant | $\forall i: S_i \text{ is a subset of some MST}$ | $i = 0, 1, \dots, \vert V \vert$ | 7-mark proof | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Tables:** In all KTU exam answers, when you need to express "absolute value" or "divides" inside a table row, **always** use $\vert x \vert$ rendered via `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`. The raw pipe character $\vert$ breaks markdown table parsing. ## 2.3 Engineering & Real-World Utility Prim's algorithm is the workhorse behind a vast array of engineering problems: - **Telecommunications Network Design:** Laying minimum-cost fiber-optic cables between cities. - **VLSI Circuit Layout:** Connecting millions of transistors on a chip with minimum wire length. - **Transportation Planning:** Designing road/rail networks with minimum construction cost. - **Cluster Analysis:** Single-linkage clustering in data mining builds MSTs. - **Approximation Algorithms:** TSP tour approximation uses $2 \times$ MST weight as a lower bound. - **Image Segmentation:** Minimum spanning forests partition images for computer vision tasks. > [!TIP] > **Industry Insight:** Modern network design tools (e.g., Cisco's network simulation, Amazon's AWS backbone) use variants of MST algorithms to optimize routing topology. The difference between a $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ and $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ implementation becomes significant at the scale of millions of nodes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Graph for Analytical Trace We use a **canonical KTU textbook example** with 5 vertices and 7 edges. The graph is undirected and weighted: | Edge | Weight $w$ | | :--- | :--- | | $(A, B)$ | 2 | | $(A, C)$ | 3 | | $(B, C)$ | 1 | | $(B, D)$ | 4 | | $(C, D)$ | 5 | | $(C, E)$ | 6 | | $(D, E)$ | 7 | **Expected Output (validation target):** MST edges = $\{(A,B), (B,C), (B,D), (C,E)\}$, total weight $= 2 + 1 + 4 + 6 = 13$. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution Trace We maintain the following data structures at every step: - $S$ — set of vertices **already in** the MST. - $key[v]$ — minimum weight of an edge connecting $v$ to $S$; $\infty$ if $v \notin S$ has no such edge. - $\pi[v]$ — parent of $v$ in the MST (the vertex in $S$ from which $v$ is connected). **Initial State:** $S = \emptyset$, $key[v] = \infty$ for all $v$, $\pi[v] = \text{NIL}$. **Step 0 — Initialize from source vertex $A$:** We pick an arbitrary starting vertex (the algorithm works regardless of choice). Let $s = A$. $$key[A] = 0, \quad \pi[A] = \text{NIL}$$ **Data Structure State (Priority Queue Q ordered by key):** - $Q = \{ (A, 0), (B, \infty), (C, \infty), (D, \infty), (E, \infty) \}$ --- **Step 1 — Extract-Min from $Q$:** The vertex with minimum $key$ is $A$ (key = 0). Extract $A$ and add to $S$. $$S = \{A\}$$ Update $key$ and $\pi$ for all neighbours of $A$: - Neighbour $B$ (via edge $A-B$ with weight 2): $key[B] = 2$, $\pi[B] = A$. - Neighbour $C$ (via edge $A-C$ with weight 3): $key[C] = 3$, $\pi[C] = A$. **Updated Q:** $\{ (B, 2, A), (C, 3, A), (D, \infty, -), (E, \infty, -) \}$ --- **Step 2 — Extract-Min from $Q$:** The vertex with minimum $key$ is $B$ (key = 2). Extract $B$ and add to $S$. $$S = \{A, B\}$$ **MST so far:** $\{(A, B, 2)\}$, total weight $= 2$. Update $key$ and $\pi$ for all neighbours of $B$ that are not yet in $S$: - Neighbour $C$ (via edge $B-C$ with weight 1): current $key[C] = 3$. New candidate weight $= 1 < 3$. Therefore $key[C] = 1$, $\pi[C] = B$. - Neighbour $D$ (via edge $B-D$ with weight 4): current $key[D] = \infty$. New $key[D] = 4$, $\pi[D] = B$. **Updated Q:** $\{ (C, 1, B), (D, 4, B), (E, \infty, -) \}$ --- **Step 3 — Extract-Min from $Q$:** The vertex with minimum $key$ is $C$ (key = 1). Extract $C$ and add to $S$. $$S = \{A, B, C\}$$ **MST so far:** $\{(A, B, 2), (B, C, 1)\}$, total weight $= 3$. Update $key$ and $\pi$ for all neighbours of $C$ that are not yet in $S$: - Neighbour $D$ (via edge $C-D$ with weight 5): current $key[D] = 4$. New candidate weight $= 5 > 4$. **No update** (Relaxation fails). - Neighbour $E$ (via edge $C-E$ with weight 6): current $key[E] = \infty$. New $key[E] = 6$, $\pi[E] = C$. **Updated Q:** $\{ (D, 4, B), (E, 6, C) \}$ --- **Step 4 — Extract-Min from $Q$:** The vertex with minimum $key$ is $D$ (key = 4). Extract $D$ and add to $S$. $$S = \{A, B, C, D\}$$ **MST so far:** $\{(A, B, 2), (B, C, 1), (B, D, 4)\}$, total weight $= 7$. Update $key$ and $\pi$ for all neighbours of $D$ that are not yet in $S$: - Neighbour $E$ (via edge $D-E$ with weight 7): current $key[E] = 6$. New candidate weight $= 7 > 6$. **No update** (Relaxation fails). **Updated Q:** $\{ (E, 6, C) \}$ --- **Step 5 — Extract-Min from $Q$:** The vertex with minimum $key$ is $E$ (key = 6). Extract $E$ and add to $S$. $$S = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$$ **MST so far:** $\{(A, B, 2), (B, C, 1), (B, D, 4), (C, E, 6)\}$, total weight $= 13$. **Q is empty → Algorithm terminates.** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Complete State Transition Table The following single matrix encapsulates every state transition, suitable for direct reproduction in a KTU answer sheet: | Iteration $i$ | Extracted Vertex $u$ | $S$ after insertion | Edge added to MST | $key[B], key[C], key[D], key[E]$ | $\pi[B], \pi[C], \pi[D], \pi[E]$ | | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $A$ | $\{A\}$ | — (root) | $2, 3, \infty, \infty$ | $A, A, -, -$ | | 2 | $B$ | $\{A, B\}$ | $(A, B, 2)$ | $2, 1, 4, \infty$ | $A, B, B, -$ | | 3 | $C$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | $(B, C, 1)$ | $2, 1, 4, 6$ | $A, B, B, C$ | | 4 | $D$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | $(B, D, 4)$ | $2, 1, 4, 6$ | $A, B, B, C$ | | 5 | $E$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | $(C, E, 6)$ | $2, 1, 4, 6$ | $A, B, B, C$ | ## 3.4 Mathematical Verification of Total Weight We can formally verify the MST weight using the cut property at each iteration. Define $C_i = (S_i, V \setminus S_i)$ as the cut at step $i$: $$\begin{aligned} W_{MST} &= \sum_{i=1}^{\vert V \vert - 1} w(\text{edge}_i) \\ &= w(A, B) + w(B, C) + w(B, D) + w(C, E) \\ &= 2 + 1 + 4 + 6 \\ &= 13 \end{aligned}$$ **Lower bound verification (any spanning tree on 5 vertices has at least 4 edges):** $$\vert E_{MST} \vert = 5 - 1 = 4 \quad \checkmark$$ ## 3.5 Reference C Implementation (KTU Lab Standard) ```c #include #include #include #define V 5 // Number of vertices // Function to find the vertex with minimum key value int minKey(int key[], bool mstSet[]) { int min = INT_MAX, min_index = -1; for (int v = 0; v < V; v++) { if (mstSet[v] == false && key[v] < min) { min = key[v]; min_index = v; } } return min_index; } // Function to print the constructed MST stored in parent[] void printMST(int parent[], int graph[V][V]) { int totalWeight = 0; printf("Edge \tWeight\n"); for (int i = 1; i < V; i++) { printf("%d - %d \t%d \n", parent[i], i, graph[i][parent[i]]); totalWeight += graph[i][parent[i]]; } printf("Total MST Weight: %d\n", totalWeight); } // Prim's Algorithm using adjacency matrix void primMST(int graph[V][V]) { int parent[V]; // Array to store constructed MST int key[V]; // Key values used to pick minimum weight edge bool mstSet[V]; // To represent set of vertices included in MST // Initialize all keys as INFINITE and mstSet[] as false for (int i = 0; i < V; i++) { key[i] = INT_MAX; mstSet[i] = false; } // Always include first vertex in MST key[0] = 0; // Make key 0 so that this vertex is picked first parent[0] = -1; // First node is always root of MST // The MST will have V vertices for (int count = 0; count < V - 1; count++) { // Pick the minimum key vertex from the set of vertices not yet in MST int u = minKey(key, mstSet); mstSet[u] = true; // Add the picked vertex to the MST set // Update key value and parent index of the adjacent vertices for (int v = 0; v < V; v++) { // graph[u][v] is non-zero only for adjacent vertices // mstSet[v] is false for vertices not yet included in MST // Update the key only if graph[u][v] is smaller than key[v] if (graph[u][v] && mstSet[v] == false && graph[u][v] < key[v]) { parent[v] = u; key[v] = graph[u][v]; } } } // Print the constructed MST printMST(parent, graph); } int main() { /* Example graph used in our analytical trace Vertices: 0=A, 1=B, 2=C, 3=D, 4=E */ int graph[V][V] = { {0, 2, 3, 0, 0}, {2, 0, 1, 4, 0}, {3, 1, 0, 5, 6}, {0, 4, 5, 0, 7}, {0, 0, 6, 7, 0} }; primMST(graph); return 0; } ``` **Expected Output of the C Program:** ``` Edge Weight 0 - 1 2 1 - 2 1 1 - 3 4 2 - 4 6 Total MST Weight: 13 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Code Reading Tip for KTU Lab Viva:** The `minKey` function scans the entire key array — this is the source of the $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ complexity. The inner loop runs $V$ times, and the outer loop also runs $V$ times, giving $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ per Prim's execution. A binary-heap version replaces `minKey` with `extract-min` and the inner loop with `decrease-key`, both taking $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$, giving $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ overall. ## 3.6 Hand-Traceable Dry Run of the C Code The following dry-run maps the C code's variables to our analytical trace. This is **exactly the kind of working** KTU lab evaluators expect during your record submission: | Iteration `count` | `u = minKey()` | `mstSet[u] = true` | `parent[]` updated to | `key[]` final state | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 0 | $A$ (key 0) | $A \rightarrow$ true | $parent[B]=A, parent[C]=A$ | $[0, 2, 3, \infty, \infty]$ | | 1 | $B$ (key 2) | $B \rightarrow$ true | $parent[C]=B, parent[D]=B$ | $[0, 2, 1, 4, \infty]$ | | 2 | $C$ (key 1) | $C \rightarrow$ true | $parent[E]=C$ | $[0, 2, 1, 4, 6]$ | | 3 | $D$ (key 4) | $D \rightarrow$ true | (no update, $key[E]=6 < 7$) | $[0, 2, 1, 4, 6]$ | | 4 | $E$ (key 6) | $E \rightarrow$ true | — | $[0, 2, 1, 4, 6]$ | > [!TIP] > **Why does iteration 3 leave `key[E] = 6` unchanged?** Because the relaxation check `graph[D][E] < key[E]` becomes `7 < 6`, which is **false**. The code's `if` block is skipped, preserving the previously chosen edge $(C, E, 6)$ over the rejected $(D, E, 7)$. This is a **textbook example of the cut property in action**. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Flowchart — High-Level Logic of Prim's Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start primMST]) --> initA["Initialize key all INFINITE
parent all NIL
mstSet all false
key of source = 0"] initA --> loopA{"for count = 0 to V-1"} loopA --> pickA["u = Extract-Min from key array
(vertex not in mstSet with min key)"] pickA --> includeA["Add u to mstSet
mstSet u = true"] includeA --> relaxA["For every v adjacent to u and v not in mstSet:
if w u v less than key v then
parent v = u
key v = w u v"] relaxA --> checkA{"count less than V - 1"} checkA -->|Yes| loopA checkA -->|No| printA["Print MST edges from parent array
Print total weight"] printA --> stopA([End]) ``` ## 4.2 Reference Graph Visualization (Sample Problem) ```mermaid graph TD nodeA((A)) ---|2| nodeB((B)) nodeA ---|3| nodeC((C)) nodeB ---|1| nodeC nodeB ---|4| nodeD((D)) nodeC ---|5| nodeD nodeC ---|6| nodeE((E)) nodeD ---|7| nodeE ``` ## 4.3 Final MST Visualization (Highlighted Edges) The edges marked in **bold double lines** form the Minimum Spanning Tree. The thin edges are non-tree edges that were rejected by the cut property: ```mermaid graph TD nodeA((A)) ==2==> nodeB((B)) nodeA ---|3| nodeC((C)) nodeB ==1==> nodeC nodeB ==4==> nodeD((D)) nodeC ---|5| nodeD nodeC ==6==> nodeE((E)) nodeD ---|7| nodeE ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture The following architecture diagram maps the **five functional blocks** of the algorithm to their roles — useful for KTU lab viva when asked *"Explain the working of your program"*. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["INPUT LAYER"] block1["Adjacency Matrix

Each edge causes 1 decrease-key of O log V
Total O E log V"] q1 -->|Adjacency List + Fibonacci Heap| fib["Decrease key becomes O 1 amortised
Extract min becomes O log V amortised

(-2 marks) > 2. **Updating `key[v]` without first checking `mstSet[v] == false`** — you may overwrite an already-finalized vertex. (-2 marks) > 3. **Confusing Prim's with Kruskal's** in viva — remember: Prim's grows from **one vertex**, Kruskal's picks **globally minimum edges**. (-1 to -3 marks) > 4. **Failing to state the cut property** in the "why greedy works" question — examiners explicitly allocate 2-3 marks for this justification. (-2 marks) > 5. **Off-by-one in edge count** — MST has $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges, NOT $\vert V \vert$ or $\vert V \vert - 2$. (-1 mark) --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Minimum Spanning Tree (MST). State and briefly explain the cut property used in Prim's algorithm.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)** of a connected, weighted, undirected graph $G = (V, E, w)$ is a spanning subgraph $T = (V, E')$ with $\vert E' \vert = \vert V \vert - 1$, no cycles, and **minimum total edge weight** $W(T) = \sum_{e \in E'} w(e)$. **Cut Property:** For any cut $(S, V \setminus S)$ of $G$, the **light edge** — defined as the edge with minimum weight crossing the cut — belongs to **every MST** of $G$. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for cut property statement, 1 mark for "light edge" explanation.) --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Compare Prim's algorithm and Kruskal's algorithm in terms of strategy, time complexity, and ideal use case.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Prim's Algorithm | Kruskal's Algorithm | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Strategy | Vertex-growth (one tree) | Edge-growth (forest) | | Data structure | Priority queue / key array | Sorted edge list + Union-Find | | Time complexity (matrix) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert E \vert)$ | | Time complexity (heap) | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert E \vert)$ | | Ideal for | Dense graphs | Sparse graphs | (1 mark for strategy, 1 mark for complexity, 1 mark for use case.) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 marks) **(a)** Trace Prim's algorithm on the following graph starting from vertex $A$. Show the state of $key$ and $parent$ arrays after each iteration. Draw the final MST and compute its total weight. **(7 marks)** | Edge | Weight | | :--- | :--- | | $(A, B)$ | 4 | | $(A, H)$ | 8 | | $(B, C)$ | 8 | | $(B, H)$ | 11 | | $(C, D)$ | 7 | | $(C, I)$ | 2 | | $(C, F)$ | 4 | | $(D, E)$ | 9 | | $(D, F)$ | 14 | | $(E, F)$ | 10 | | $(F, G)$ | 2 | | $(G, H)$ | 1 | | $(G, I)$ | 6 | | $(H, I)$ | 7 | **(b)** Write the algorithm (pseudocode) for Prim's algorithm and analyze its time complexity. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for B-A(a):** **Step 1:** Start with $A$. $key[A] = 0$, all others $\infty$. $S = \{A\}$. Update: $key[B]=4, key[H]=8$. **Step 2:** Extract $B$ (key 4). $S = \{A, B\}$. Update: $key[C]=8$ (from $B-C$), $key[H]$ stays $8$ (since $w(B,H)=11 > 8$). **Step 3:** Tie between $C$ and $H$ (both key 8). Per convention, pick $C$ (alphabetic). $S = \{A, B, C\}$. Update: $key[D]=7, key[I]=2, key[F]=4$. **Step 4:** Extract $I$ (key 2). $S = \{A, B, C, I\}$. Update: $key[F]$ stays 4 (since $w(C,F)=4$ is already better than $w(I,F)$ — wait, $I$ is not adjacent to $F$ here, so only neighbour is $G$ via $w(G,I)=6$, and $H$ via $w(H,I)=7$). $key[G]=6, key[H]$ stays $8$ (since $7 < 8$). So $key[H]=7, key[G]=6$. **Step 5:** Extract $F$ (key 4). $S = \{A, B, C, I, F\}$. Update: $key[E]=10$ (via $F$), $key[G]$ becomes $\min(6, 2) = 2$. **Step 6:** Extract $G$ (key 2). $S = \{A, B, C, I, F, G\}$. Update: $key[H]$ becomes $\min(7, 1) = 1$. **Step 7:** Extract $H$ (key 1). $S = \{A, B, C, I, F, G, H\}$. Update: $key[E]$ stays $10$. **Step 8:** Extract $E$ (key 9 via $D-E$, but $E$ is at key 10 from $F$; $D$ not yet in $S$). Wait — we need to check. After step 7, $S$ does not contain $D$ or $E$. The candidates: $D$ (key 7 from $C-D$) and $E$ (key 10 from $F-E$). Extract $D$. **Step 8 (corrected):** Extract $D$ (key 7). $S = \{A, B, C, I, F, G, H, D\}$. Update: $key[E]=9$ (via $D-E$), $key[F]$ already final. **Step 9:** Extract $E$ (key 9). $S = \{A, B, C, I, F, G, H, D, E\}$. **Final MST edges:** $(A,B,4), (B,C,8), (C,I,2), (C,F,4), (F,G,2), (G,H,1), (C,D,7), (D,E,9)$. **Total MST Weight:** $4 + 8 + 2 + 4 + 2 + 1 + 7 + 9 = 37$. > [!TIP] > **Mark Allocation Hint (7 marks for part a):** 1 mark per correct extraction step (5 marks total for the table), 1 mark for the final MST list, 1 mark for the total weight. #### **Model Solution for B-A(b):** ``` ALGORITHM Prim(G, w, r): // G = (V, E) weighted graph, w = weight function, r = root vertex for each vertex u in V do key[u] = INFINITY parent[u] = NIL end for key[r] = 0 Q = priority queue containing all vertices, keyed by key[] while Q is not empty do u = Extract-Min(Q) for each vertex v in Adj[u] do if v in Q and w(u, v) < key[v] then parent[v] = u key[v] = w(u, v) end if end for end while END PRIM ``` **Time Complexity Analysis:** The total running time has three components: $$\begin{aligned} T(n) &= T_{\text{init}} + T_{\text{extract}} + T_{\text{relax}} \\ &= \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert) + (\vert V \vert) \cdot T_{\text{extract-min}} + (\vert E \vert) \cdot T_{\text{decrease-key}} \end{aligned}$$ For an adjacency matrix with a linear scan (no heap): - $T_{\text{extract-min}} = \mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert)$ (linear scan over $V$ keys). - $T_{\text{decrease-key}} = \mathcal{O}(1)$ (direct array assignment). - Total: $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert^2)$. For a binary heap with adjacency list: - $T_{\text{extract-min}} = \mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$. - $T_{\text{decrease-key}} = \mathcal{O}(\log \vert V \vert)$. - Total: $\mathcal{O}((\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) \log \vert V \vert) = \mathcal{O}(\vert E \vert \log \vert V \vert)$. > [!TIP] > **Mark Allocation (7 marks for part b):** 2 marks for clean pseudocode, 2 marks for the iteration loop, 2 marks for the time complexity derivation, 1 mark for stating the final asymptotic bound. --- ### **Question B-B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 marks) **(a)** Consider the following graph with 6 vertices and the given edge weights. Run Prim's algorithm starting from vertex $V_1$. Show all intermediate steps and the final MST. **(7 marks)** | Edge | Weight | | :--- | :--- | | $(V_1, V_2)$ | 5 | | $(V_1, V_3)$ | 2 | | $(V_1, V_4)$ | 4 | | $(V_2, V_3)$ | 6 | | $(V_2, V_5)$ | 3 | | $(V_3, V_4)$ | 1 | | $(V_3, V_5)$ | 7 | | $(V_3, V_6)$ | 8 | | $(V_4, V_6)$ | 5 | | $(V_5, V_6)$ | 4 | **(b)** Explain the difference between the **adjacency matrix** implementation and the **adjacency list + binary heap** implementation of Prim's algorithm. When would you prefer one over the other? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for B-B(a):** **Step 1:** $S = \{V_1\}$. $key[V_1] = 0$. Update neighbours: $key[V_2]=5, key[V_3]=2, key[V_4]=4$. **Step 2:** Extract $V_3$ (key 2). $S = \{V_1, V_3\}$. MST: $(V_1, V_3, 2)$. Update: $key[V_2]$ becomes $\min(5, 6) = 5$ (no change), $key[V_4]$ becomes $\min(4, 1) = 1$, $key[V_5] = 7$, $key[V_6] = 8$. **Step 3:** Extract $V_4$ (key 1). $S = \{V_1, V_3, V_4\}$. MST: $(V_3, V_4, 1)$. Update: $key[V_6]$ becomes $\min(8, 5) = 5$. **Step 4:** Extract $V_2$ (key 5, tied with $V_6$). Per convention, pick $V_2$. $S = \{V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4\}$. MST: $(V_1, V_2, 5)$. Update: $key[V_5]$ becomes $\min(7, 3) = 3$. **Step 5:** Extract $V_5$ (key 3). $S = \{V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4, V_5\}$. MST: $(V_2, V_5, 3)$. Update: $key[V_6]$ becomes $\min(5, 4) = 4$. **Step 6:** Extract $V_6$ (key 4). $S = \{V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4, V_5, V_6\}$. MST: $(V_5, V_6, 4)$. Done. **Final MST edges:** $(V_1, V_3, 2), (V_3, V_4, 1), (V_1, V_2, 5), (V_2, V_5, 3), (V_5, V_6, 4)$. **Total MST Weight:** $2 + 1 + 5 + 3 + 4 = 15$. --- #### **Model Solution for B-B(b):** **Adjacency Matrix Implementation:** - Graph stored as $V \times V$ 2D array. $w(u, v)$ accessible in $\mathcal{O}(1)$. - `minKey()` scans all $V$ vertices in $\mathcal{O}(V)$ to find the minimum. - The outer loop runs $V$ times, and the inner relaxation also loops $V$ times. - Total: $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ time, $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ space. **Adjacency List + Binary Heap Implementation:** - Graph stored as an array of $V$ linked lists, one per vertex. $w(u, v)$ accessible in $\mathcal{O}(degree(u))$. - `extract-min()` on a binary heap of size $V$ takes $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$. - `decrease-key()` also takes $\mathcal{O}(\log V)$. - Each edge is examined once; hence the total relaxation cost is $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$. - Total: $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ time, $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ space. **When to prefer which:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Matrix} &: \text{Prefer when } \vert E \vert \approx \vert V \vert^2 \text{ (dense graph)} \\ \text{Heap} &: \text{Prefer when } \vert E \vert \ll \vert V \vert^2 \text{ (sparse graph)} \end{aligned}$$ **Rule of thumb:** If the graph has $|E| > |V|^2 / \log |V|$, the matrix version is asymptotically faster. Otherwise, the heap version wins. In KTU lab questions, the matrix version is the default expected implementation unless otherwise stated. > [!TIP] > **Mark Allocation (7 marks for part b):** 2 marks for matrix version explanation, 2 marks for heap version explanation, 2 marks for complexity comparison, 1 mark for the selection criterion with a concrete example. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** MST is a spanning tree of minimum total weight; it always has exactly $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. - **Core Idea:** Prim's grows the MST one vertex at a time, always picking the minimum-weight edge crossing the **cut** between the MST and the rest of the graph. - **Cut Property:** Every light edge (minimum weight) across a cut belongs to every MST. This is the theoretical foundation of Prim's correctness. - **Greedy Choice:** Local minimum = global minimum, because at every step the chosen edge is provably part of some (and in fact every) MST. - **Initialisation:** $key[\text{root}] = 0$, all other $key[v] = \infty$, all $parent[v] = \text{NIL}$, all $mstSet[v] = \text{false}$. - **Main Loop (runs $V - 1$ times):** 1. Extract the vertex $u$ with minimum $key$ not in $mstSet$. 2. Mark $mstSet[u] = \text{true}$. 3. For every neighbour $v$ of $u$ not in $mstSet$: if $w(u, v) < key[v]$, update $key[v] = w(u, v)$ and $parent[v] = u$. - **Output:** The `parent[]` array encodes the MST; iterate from index 1 to $V-1$ to print the edges. - **Time Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ for matrix; $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ for binary heap; $\mathcal{O}(E + V \log V)$ for Fibonacci heap. - **Space Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(V + E)$ for adjacency list; $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ for adjacency matrix storage. - **Connectivity Assumption:** Prim's algorithm **requires the graph to be connected**. If the input is disconnected, the algorithm still runs but the resulting structure is a **Minimum Spanning Forest** (one tree per connected component). - **Versus Kruskal's:** Prim's = vertex-growth + priority queue; Kruskal's = edge-growth + Union-Find. Prim's is preferred for dense graphs; Kruskal's is preferred for sparse graphs. - **Versus Dijkstra's:** Both use priority queues, but Prim's uses **edge weight** as the key, while Dijkstra's uses **path distance from source**. The two algorithms diverge on graphs with negative edges — Dijkstra's fails, but Prim's is unaffected. - **Common Viva Question:** *"Does Prim's algorithm work for directed graphs?"* — **No**, it is defined only for undirected graphs. For directed graphs, the analogous problem is the **Minimum Cost Arborescence** (Edmonds' / Chu-Liu algorithm). - **Determinism Note:** The MST is **not unique** when the graph has multiple edges of the same weight crossing the same cut. Prim's breaks ties according to the data structure's internal ordering (e.g., insertion order or vertex index). - **Lab Viva One-Liner:** "Prim's algorithm builds the MST by **always extending the frontier with the cheapest available edge**, a strategy that the cut property guarantees will never lock us out of the global optimum." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU 2024 Exam Mantra:** *MST = $\vert V \vert - 1$ edges. Cut property justifies the greedy choice. $\mathcal{O}(V^2)$ with matrix, $\mathcal{O}(E \log V)$ with heap. Always reset $key = \infty$ and $mstSet = \text{false}$ before starting. The final tree is reconstructed by walking the $parent[]$ array from index 1 to $V-1$.*

topics.title: Implement Dynamic Programming Algorithm to Find the Solution to Travelling Salesman Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL508 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Dynamic Programming Algorithm to Find the Solution to Travelling Salesman Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem in the class **NP-Hard**. Given a set of $n$ cities and the pairwise cost (or distance) between every pair of cities, the objective is to find the **shortest possible Hamiltonian cycle** — a closed tour that:

1. Starts at a designated source city (conventionally city $0$),
2. Visits **every other city exactly once**, and
3. Returns to the starting city.

Formally, for a complete weighted graph $G = (V, E, W)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, we seek a permutation $\pi$ of the cities that minimizes:

$$\text{Cost}(\pi) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} W\bigl[\pi(i),\, \pi(i{+}1)\bigr] \;+\; W\bigl[\pi(n),\, \pi(1)\bigr]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"TSP is an optimization problem that asks for the shortest Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph. Its decision version is NP-Complete, and its optimization version is NP-Hard."*

## 1.2 The Dynamic Programming Solution — Held–Karp Algorithm

The **Held–Karp algorithm** (1962) solves TSP exactly in **pseudo-polynomial time** using Dynamic Programming. The key insight is that even though there are $(n-1)!$ possible tours, the number of *distinct subproblems* encountered while solving TSP is much smaller, allowing memoization to eliminate redundant computation.

> [!NOTE]
> The DP formulation of TSP is part of the **Karp's 21 NP-Complete Problems** family and is a **mandatory experiment** in the PCITL508 syllabus under Module 2.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Forgetful Pilot"

Imagine a **pilot** planning a round-the-world fuel-efficient route touching **$n$ airports** and returning home. The brute-force pilot would list all $(n-1)!$ permutations, fly a mental flight for each, and pick the cheapest — astronomically slow for $n \geq 15$.

The **DP pilot** is smarter. At every city, she asks a more focused question:
> *"Given that I still need to visit the cities in **set S**, and my next landing is **city i**, what is the cheapest way to do this?"*

She writes this answer in a **notebook (memoization table)**. Whenever the same question arises again, she just **looks it up** instead of re-computing. The notebook has only $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ entries — a **massive saving** over the $(n-1)!$ permutations.

**Why this works:** Many tours share the same *suffix* (e.g., "starting from a 3-city subset, ending at city $i$"). The DP pilot recognizes these as the **same subproblem** and solves each only once.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Used in TSP-DP

- **Number of subproblems:** Exactly $\boldsymbol{n \cdot 2^{n}}$ entries in the DP table.
- **Time per subproblem:** Up to $\boldsymbol{O(n)}$ transitions to evaluate.
- **Total time complexity:** $\boldsymbol{O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})}$.
- **Space complexity:** $\boldsymbol{O(n \cdot 2^{n})}$.
- **Source city:** Always **0** (fixed, by convention in KTU examinations).
- **Distances:** $W[i][i] = 0$ (diagonal of cost matrix), symmetric matrix $W[i][j] = W[j][i]$ is the **default assumption** unless stated otherwise.

> [!TIP]
> KTU exam questions often explicitly say *"assume a complete graph"* and *"distances are symmetric."* Always read the matrix carefully — sometimes a question provides an **asymmetric** matrix to test understanding.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 State Definition — The Heart of the DP

Define the DP state as a function of two parameters:

$$C(S, i) \;=\; \text{minimum cost of a path that:}$$

- **Starts** at the source city $0$,
- **Visits every city in set $S$ exactly once**, in any order,
- **Ends** at city $i$, where $i \in S$,
- and where $0 \notin S$.

Here, $S$ is a non-empty subset of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$, represented as a **bitmask** of length $n-1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exclude city 0 from $S$?**
> City 0 is fixed as the starting point. Including it in $S$ would create ambiguity and violate the "exactly once" constraint — city 0 is visited twice (start and end), so it cannot also be a "middle" city.

## 2.2 The Recurrence Relation — Step-by-Step Logic

### Base Case (Subproblem size 1)

When $S$ contains exactly one city, the path is trivially the direct edge from city 0 to that city:

$$C\bigl(\{i\},\, i\bigr) \;=\; W[0][i] \quad \text{for all } i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$$

### Recursive Case (Subproblem size $\geq 2$)

For a state $C(S, i)$ where $\vert S \vert \geq 2$, the last move before reaching $i$ must have come from some city $j$ that was the **second-to-last city**. So we try all such $j$:

$$C(S, i) \;=\; \min_{j \in S,\; j \neq i} \Bigl\{ C\bigl(S \setminus \{i\},\, j\bigr) \;+\; W[j][i] \Bigr\}$$

### Final Answer — Closing the Tour

The optimal tour starts at $0$, visits all other cities in some order, and **returns to $0$**. So the final answer comes from picking the best "last city before returning":

$$\text{OPT} \;=\; \min_{i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}} \Bigl\{ C\bigl(\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\},\, i\bigr) \;+\; W[i][0] \Bigr\}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Description / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|
| **DP State** | $C(S, i)$ | Min cost to traverse set $S$, ending at $i$ |
| **State Space** | $n \cdot 2^{n}$ | Total DP table entries (bitmask $\times$ endpoint) |
| **Base Case** | $C(\{i\}, i) = W[0][i]$ | Direct cost from source to a single city |
| **Recurrence** | $C(S, i) = \min_{j} \{ C(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + W[j][i] \}$ | Try all possible predecessors $j$ |
| **Final Cost** | $\min_{i} \{ C(\{1..n-1\}, i) + W[i][0] \}$ | Best "return-to-source" edge |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ | Exponential, but vastly better than $O(n!)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ | DP table storage |
| **Brute Force Cost** | $O(n!)$ | Lists all permutations |
| **Asymmetry** | $W[i][j] \neq W[j][i]$ | Direction matters (KTU twist question) |
| **Triangular Inequality** | $W[i][k] \leq W[i][j] + W[j][k]$ | Holds for true Euclidean distances |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $\vert S \vert$ with $n$ in the recurrence. The number of recursive calls is governed by the size of $S$, not $n$. The total subproblems across all $S$ sizes is $2^{n} - 1$ subsets $\times$ at most $n$ endpoints.

## 2.4 Why "Pseudo-Polynomial" — KTU Favorite Question

TSP-DP has complexity $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ which is **exponential in $n$** (input size = $\log_{2} W_{\max}$ for the weights, or simply $n$ as a parameter). Thus it is **not polynomial** in the input length. However, it is **polynomial in the value $2^{n}$** — hence the term *pseudo-polynomial*. This distinction is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application Domain | Use of TSP-DP |
|---|---|
| **VLSI Chip Design** | Optimal drill path for PCB hole punching |
| **Logistics & Supply Chain** | Delivery route optimization for fleets |
| **Genomic Sequencing** | DNA fragment assembly ordering |
| **Robotics & Manufacturing** | Minimizing tool-change time on CNC machines |
| **Astronomy** | Telescope slew-time minimization for sky surveys |
| **Network Packet Routing** | Circuit-switched network call routing |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Example

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (Classic 4-City KTU-Style Problem)

### Given Data

A salesman starts from city **0** and must visit cities **1, 2, 3** exactly once each, then return to **0**. The symmetric distance matrix $W$ is:

$$W \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> Matrix entry $W[i][j]$ gives the cost to travel directly from city $i$ to city $j$. Note $W[i][i]=0$ and $W[i][j]=W[j][i]$.

### Stage 1 — Base Case ($\vert S \vert = 1$)

For each single-city subset, the cost is just the direct edge from city 0:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1\}, 1) &= W[0][1] = 10 \\
C(\{2\}, 2) &= W[0][2] = 15 \\
C(\{3\}, 3) &= W[0][3] = 20
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stage 2 — Subproblem Size $\vert S \vert = 2$

Apply the recurrence $C(S, i) = C(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + W[j][i]$ for each $(S, i)$ pair.

**Subset $\{1, 2\}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2\}, 1) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + W[2][1] = 15 + 35 = \mathbf{50} \\
C(\{1, 2\}, 2) &= C(\{1\}, 1) + W[1][2] = 10 + 35 = \mathbf{45}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subset $\{1, 3\}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 3\}, 1) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + W[3][1] = 20 + 25 = \mathbf{45} \\
C(\{1, 3\}, 3) &= C(\{1\}, 1) + W[1][3] = 10 + 25 = \mathbf{35}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Subset $\{2, 3\}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{2, 3\}, 2) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + W[3][2] = 20 + 30 = \mathbf{50} \\
C(\{2, 3\}, 3) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + W[2][3] = 15 + 30 = \mathbf{45}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Stage 3 — Subproblem Size $\vert S \vert = 3$ (the full set $\{1, 2, 3\}$)

Now we consider ending at each city after visiting all three:

**End at city 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 1) &= \min \begin{cases} C(\{2, 3\}, 2) + W[2][1] \\ C(\{2, 3\}, 3) + W[3][1] \end{cases} \\
&= \min \begin{cases} 50 + 35 = 85 \\ 45 + 25 = 70 \end{cases} \;=\; \mathbf{70} \quad \text{(via city 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**End at city 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 2) &= \min \begin{cases} C(\{1, 3\}, 1) + W[1][2] \\ C(\{1, 3\}, 3) + W[3][2] \end{cases} \\
&= \min \begin{cases} 45 + 35 = 80 \\ 35 + 30 = 65 \end{cases} \;=\; \mathbf{65} \quad \text{(via city 3)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**End at city 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 3) &= \min \begin{cases} C(\{1, 2\}, 1) + W[1][3] \\ C(\{1, 2\}, 2) + W[2][3] \end{cases} \\
&= \min \begin{cases} 50 + 25 = 75 \\ 45 + 30 = 75 \end{cases} \;=\; \mathbf{75} \quad \text{(tie, both predecessors)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Stage — Closing the Tour (Return to City 0)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OPT} &= \min_{i \in \{1, 2, 3\}} \bigl\{ C(\{1, 2, 3\}, i) + W[i][0] \bigr\} \\
&= \min \begin{cases} C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 1) + W[1][0] = 70 + 10 = 80 \\ C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 2) + W[2][0] = 65 + 15 = 80 \\ C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 3) + W[3][0] = 75 + 20 = 95 \end{cases} \\
&= \mathbf{80}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Path Reconstruction (Tracing Back the Choices)

To recover the **actual tour** (not just its cost), we backtrack using stored predecessor pointers:

| Step | State | Chosen Predecessor | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Final: $i=1$ (tied with $i=2$, pick $i=1$) | — | $\text{OPT}=80$ via city 1 |
| 2 | $C(\{1,2,3\}, 1)=70$ via $j=3$ | City **3** | $45 + 25 = 70$ |
| 3 | $C(\{2,3\}, 3)=45$ via $j=2$ | City **2** | $15 + 30 = 45$ |
| 4 | $C(\{2\}, 2)=15$ via $j=0$ (base) | City **0** | Base case reached |

**Reconstructed Tour:** $\mathbf{0 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1 \to 0}$

**Verification of Cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= W[0][2] + W[2][3] + W[3][1] + W[1][0] \\
&= 15 + 30 + 25 + 10 \\
&= \mathbf{80} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Trick:** Many students compute the optimal cost but **forget to reconstruct the path**. Always show path reconstruction in the answer for full marks.

## 3.3 Complexity Derivation (Formal)

- **Number of subsets $S$:** Every non-empty subset of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ — total $2^{n-1}$ subsets, often rounded to $2^{n}$ for big-O.
- **Endpoints per subset:** At most $n$ choices of $i$.
- **Total DP cells:** $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$.
- **Transitions per cell:** Up to $n$ (loop over predecessors $j$).
- **Total time:** $O(n \cdot 2^{n} \cdot n) = O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$.

For $n = 20$ cities: brute force needs $20! \approx 2.4 \times 10^{18}$ operations, but DP needs only $(20)^{2} \cdot 2^{20} \approx 4.2 \times 10^{8}$ — a **billion-fold speedup** in practice.

## 3.4 Reference C Implementation (Lab-Ready)

```c
#include <stdio.h>
#include <limits.h>

#define MAXN 15
#define INF  999999

int n;                              /* number of cities */
int dist[MAXN][MAXN];               /* distance / cost matrix */
int dp[1 << MAXN][MAXN];            /* DP table: dp[mask][i] */
int parent[1 << MAXN][MAXN];        /* for path reconstruction */
int VISITED_ALL;                    /* bitmask with first n bits = 1 */

int tsp(int mask, int pos) {
    if (mask == VISITED_ALL)
        return dist[pos][0];        /* return to source */

    if (dp[mask][pos] != -1)
        return dp[mask][pos];

    int ans = INF;
    int bestCity = -1;

    for (int city = 0; city < n; city++) {
        if ((mask & (1 << city)) == 0) {     /* city not yet visited */
            int newMask = mask | (1 << city);
            int candidate = dist[pos][city] + tsp(newMask, city);
            if (candidate < ans) {
                ans = candidate;
                bestCity = city;
            }
        }
    }

    dp[mask][pos] = ans;
    parent[mask][pos] = bestCity;
    return ans;
}

int main(void) {
    printf("Enter number of cities: ");
    scanf("%d", &n);

    printf("Enter the %d x %d distance matrix:\n", n, n);
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
            scanf("%d", &dist[i][j]);

    VISITED_ALL = (1 << n) - 1;

    for (int i = 0; i < (1 << n); i++)
        for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
            dp[i][j] = -1;

    int minCost = tsp(1, 0);   /* start at city 0 with only 0 visited */
    printf("\nMinimum tour cost = %d\n", minCost);

    /* Path reconstruction */
    printf("Optimal tour: 0");
    int mask = 1, pos = 0;
    while (mask != VISITED_ALL) {
        int next = parent[mask][pos];
        printf(" -> %d", next);
        mask = mask | (1 << next);
        pos = next;
    }
    printf(" -> 0\n");

    return 0;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above program uses **top-down memoized recursion**. KTU labs also accept the **bottom-up iterative** version, but the recursive form is more readable and matches the recurrence directly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — State Transition Graph for the 4-City Worked Example

The diagram below shows how the DP states for our worked example (cities 0, 1, 2, 3) depend on smaller subproblems. Each node represents a DP state $C(S, i)$ — the minimum cost of a path that **starts at 0, visits set $S$, and ends at $i$**.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start: City 0])

    s1c1[ "C of 1 , 1 = 10" ]
    s1c2[ "C of 2 , 2 = 15" ]
    s1c3[ "C of 3 , 3 = 20" ]

    s2c1[ "C of 1,2 , 1 = 50" ]
    s2c2[ "C of 1,2 , 2 = 45" ]
    s3c1[ "C of 1,3 , 1 = 45" ]
    s3c3[ "C of 1,3 , 3 = 35" ]
    s4c2[ "C of 2,3 , 2 = 50" ]
    s4c3[ "C of 2,3 , 3 = 45" ]

    s5c1[ "C of 1,2,3 , 1 = 70" ]
    s5c2[ "C of 1,2,3 , 2 = 65" ]
    s5c3[ "C of 1,2,3 , 3 = 75" ]

    end1[ "Close via city 1: 80" ]
    end2[ "Close via city 2: 80" ]
    end3[ "Close via city 3: 95" ]

    opt([OPT = 80, Tour 0 to 2 to 3 to 1 to 0])

    start --> s1c1
    start --> s1c2
    start --> s1c3

    s1c2 --> s2c1
    s1c1 --> s2c2

    s1c3 --> s3c1
    s1c1 --> s3c3

    s1c3 --> s4c2
    s1c2 --> s4c3

    s4c2 --> s5c1
    s4c3 --> s5c1
    s3c1 --> s5c2
    s3c3 --> s5c2
    s2c1 --> s5c3
    s2c2 --> s5c3

    s5c1 --> end1
    s5c2 --> end2
    s5c3 --> end3

    end1 --> opt
    end2 --> opt
    end3 --> opt
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TD
    input[/Input: Cost Matrix W of n x n/]
    init[Initialize dp table of size n times 2 to n with INFINITY]
    base[Base Case: dp of mask with only city i = W of 0 and i]
    submask[Enumerate all submasks of the full mask]
    transition[For each submask S and endpoint i, compute dp of S and i = min over j in S of dp of S minus i and j + W of j and i]
    memo[Store minimum value and predecessor city]
    finalize[Final Answer = min over i of dp of full mask and i + W of i and 0]
    trace[Backtrack via parent table to recover the actual tour]
    output[/Output: Minimum cost and optimal tour/]

    input --> init
    init --> base
    base --> submask
    submask --> transition
    transition --> memo
    memo --> submask
    memo --> finalize
    finalize --> trace
    trace --> output
```

## 4.3 Phase-by-Phase Data Flow (Textual Schematic)

```
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 1: INPUT                                              │
  │   Read n (number of cities)                                  │
  │   Read n × n symmetric distance matrix W                     │
  │   Source city is fixed as 0                                  │
  └─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 2: INITIALIZATION                                     │
  │   Compute FULL_MASK = (1 << n) - 1                           │
  │   Create dp[2^n][n] initialized to -1 (uncomputed)           │
  │   Create parent[2^n][n] for path reconstruction              │
  └─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 3: BASE CASES                                         │
  │   For each i in {1..n-1}:  dp[1<<i][i] = W[0][i]            │
  │   These are the "single-city reachable" states               │
  └─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 4: RECURSIVE / ITERATIVE FILL                         │
  │   For each mask from 0 to FULL_MASK:                         │
  │     For each i in mask (i.e., city present in mask):         │
  │       dp[mask][i] = min over j in (mask \ {i}):              │
  │                       dp[mask ^ (1<<i)][j] + W[j][i]         │
  │       parent[mask][i] = arg-min j (for backtracking)         │
  └─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 5: FINAL ANSWER                                       │
  │   minCost = min over i in {1..n-1}:                          │
  │                dp[FULL_MASK][i] + W[i][0]                    │
  └─────────────────────────┬────────────────────────────────────┘
                            │
                            ▼
  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  PHASE 6: PATH RECONSTRUCTION                                │
  │   Start at pos=0, mask={0}                                   │
  │   While mask != FULL_MASK:                                   │
  │     next = parent[mask][pos]                                 │
  │     Output next                                              │
  │     mask |= (1 << next);  pos = next                         │
  │   Output "0" (return to source)                              │
  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

### Question A1
**Q: Define the Travelling Salesman Problem. State its decision version and the corresponding complexity class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)** is a combinatorial optimization problem that, given a complete weighted graph $G = (V, E, W)$, seeks a Hamiltonian cycle of **minimum total weight** that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start.

The **decision version** of TSP asks: *"Is there a Hamiltonian cycle of total cost $\leq K$?"* This decision version is **NP-Complete**, while the optimization version is **NP-Hard**. The DP-based Held–Karp algorithm solves it in $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ time, which is still exponential but vastly faster than the brute-force $O(n!)$.

*(Examiner Note: Award 1 mark each for the optimization definition, the decision version statement, and the complexity class.)*

---

### Question A2
**Q: Compare the time complexity of brute-force TSP with the DP-based Held–Karp solution. Why is the DP solution called pseudo-polynomial?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Brute-force TSP:** Generates all $(n-1)!/2$ distinct tours (for symmetric TSP) and picks the minimum. Time complexity: $O(n!)$, which is **factorial**.
- **DP Held–Karp:** Stores subproblem results in a $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ table. Time complexity: $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$, which is **exponential in $n$**.

The DP solution is called **pseudo-polynomial** because its running time is polynomial in $2^{n}$ (a numeric value related to the input size) but exponential in the number of bits needed to represent $n$ (i.e., $\log n$). Hence, it is *not* a true polynomial-time algorithm in the classical Turing-machine sense.

*(Examiner Note: 1 mark for brute-force time, 1 mark for DP time, 1 mark for explaining pseudo-polynomial.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Solve the following Travelling Salesman Problem using Dynamic Programming. The salesman starts from city 0, visits every other city exactly once, and returns to city 0. The distance matrix is given below. Show all subproblem computations and reconstruct the optimal tour.**

$$W \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 20 & 30 & 10 \\ 20 & 0 & 15 & 25 \\ 30 & 15 & 0 & 35 \\ 10 & 25 & 35 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

#### Part (a) — Compute all DP subproblem values $C(S, i)$ [7 marks]

**Solution:**

**Stage 1 — Base case ($\vert S \vert = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1\}, 1) &= W[0][1] = 20 \\
C(\{2\}, 2) &= W[0][2] = 30 \\
C(\{3\}, 3) &= W[0][3] = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 — Subproblem size 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2\}, 1) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + W[2][1] = 30 + 15 = 45 \\
C(\{1, 2\}, 2) &= C(\{1\}, 1) + W[1][2] = 20 + 15 = 35 \\
C(\{1, 3\}, 1) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + W[3][1] = 10 + 25 = 35 \\
C(\{1, 3\}, 3) &= C(\{1\}, 1) + W[1][3] = 20 + 25 = 45 \\
C(\{2, 3\}, 2) &= C(\{3\}, 3) + W[3][2] = 10 + 35 = 45 \\
C(\{2, 3\}, 3) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + W[2][3] = 30 + 35 = 65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 3 — Subproblem size 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 1) &= \min\{C(\{2,3\},2)+W[2][1],\; C(\{2,3\},3)+W[3][1]\} \\
&= \min\{45 + 15,\; 65 + 25\} = \min\{60, 90\} = 60 \quad \text{(via city 2)} \\
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 2) &= \min\{C(\{1,3\},1)+W[1][2],\; C(\{1,3\},3)+W[3][2]\} \\
&= \min\{35 + 15,\; 45 + 35\} = \min\{50, 80\} = 50 \quad \text{(via city 1)} \\
C(\{1, 2, 3\}, 3) &= \min\{C(\{1,2\},1)+W[1][3],\; C(\{1,2\},2)+W[2][3]\} \\
&= \min\{45 + 25,\; 35 + 35\} = \min\{70, 70\} = 70 \quad \text{(tie)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Compute the final answer and reconstruct the optimal tour [7 marks]

**Final Answer — Closing the tour back to city 0:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OPT} &= \min_{i \in \{1, 2, 3\}} \{C(\{1,2,3\}, i) + W[i][0]\} \\
&= \min\{60 + 20,\; 50 + 30,\; 70 + 10\} \\
&= \min\{80,\; 80,\; 80\} = \mathbf{80}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Path Reconstruction (backtrack using stored predecessors):**

| Step | Current $(S, i)$ | Chosen predecessor | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Final: pick $i = 1$ (or $i=2$ or $i=3$, all give 80) | — | All three yield $\text{OPT}=80$ |
| 2 | $C(\{1,2,3\}, 1)=60$ came from city **2** | City **2** | $C(\{2,3\}, 2)=45$ chosen |
| 3 | $C(\{2,3\}, 2)=45$ came from city **3** | City **3** | $C(\{3\}, 3)=10$ base |
| 4 | Base case reached | City **0** | Trivially |

**Reconstructed Optimal Tour:** $\mathbf{0 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1 \to 0}$ (one of multiple optimal tours)

**Verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost} &= W[0][3] + W[3][2] + W[2][1] + W[1][0] \\
&= 10 + 35 + 15 + 20 = \mathbf{80} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add the return edge** $W[i][0]$ in the final stage — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Not storing predecessor pointers** during DP fill — path reconstruction becomes impossible, costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $\min$ vs. $\arg\min$** — you need the *city* that gave the minimum, not just the value, for backtracking.
> 4. **Mixing up bitmask indexing** with vertex numbering — off-by-one errors are extremely common.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: (a) Explain the Dynamic Programming formulation of the Travelling Salesman Problem in detail, including the recurrence relation, base case, and final answer. Justify why the time complexity is $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$. (b) Write a recursive pseudo-code for TSP-DP and trace it on the 3-city graph with $W[0][1]=5$, $W[0][2]=10$, $W[1][2]=3$.**

#### Part (a) — Explanation of DP Formulation [7 marks]

**Model Solution:**

**State definition:** Let $C(S, i)$ denote the cost of the cheapest path that:
- Starts at source city $0$,
- Visits every city in set $S$ exactly once,
- Ends at city $i \in S$,
- where $S \subseteq \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ and $0 \notin S$.

**Base Case** (when $\vert S \vert = 1$, i.e., $S = \{i\}$):
$$C(\{i\}, i) = W[0][i]$$

**Recursive Case** (when $\vert S \vert \geq 2$):
$$C(S, i) = \min_{j \in S,\, j \neq i} \bigl\{ C(S \setminus \{i\},\, j) + W[j][i] \bigr\}$$

**Final Answer** (closing the tour):
$$\text{OPT} = \min_{i \in \{1..n-1\}} \bigl\{ C(\{1..n-1\},\, i) + W[i][0] \bigr\}$$

**Complexity Justification:**

- There are $2^{n-1} \approx 2^{n}$ non-empty subsets of $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$.
- For each subset $S$, we compute $C(S, i)$ for up to $n$ endpoints $i$.
- Hence the DP table has $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ cells.
- For each cell $C(S, i)$, the recurrence loops over at most $n$ predecessors $j$, performing $O(1)$ work per iteration.
- **Total time:** $O(n \cdot 2^{n} \cdot n) = O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$.
- **Space:** $O(n \cdot 2^{n})$ for the DP table.

**Why this beats brute force:** Brute force enumerates all $(n-1)!$ permutations. The DP solution observes that many permutations share the same *suffix* (set + endpoint), and computes each unique suffix only once. For $n=20$, brute force is $19! \approx 1.2 \times 10^{17}$, while DP needs only $20^{2} \cdot 2^{20} \approx 4.2 \times 10^{8}$ operations — a **speedup of 9 orders of magnitude**.

#### Part (b) — Recursive Pseudo-Code and 3-City Trace [7 marks]

**Recursive Pseudo-code (Top-Down with Memoization):**

```
FUNCTION TSP(mask, pos):
    IF mask == FULL_MASK:           // all cities visited
        RETURN dist[pos][0]          // return to source

    IF dp[mask][pos] != UNCOMPUTED:
        RETURN dp[mask][pos]         // memoized lookup

    ans = INFINITY
    FOR each city nxt from 0 to n-1:
        IF (mask & (1 << nxt)) == 0:  // nxt not yet visited
            candidate = dist[pos][nxt] + TSP(mask | (1 << nxt), nxt)
            IF candidate < ans:
                ans = candidate
                parent[mask][pos] = nxt

    dp[mask][pos] = ans
    RETURN ans
```

**Call from main:** `minCost = TSP(1, 0)` (only city 0 is "visited" initially; we start there).

**Trace on 3-City Graph** (n=3, $W[0][1]=5$, $W[0][2]=10$, $W[1][2]=3$):

Distance matrix:

$$W \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 5 & 0 & 3 \\ 10 & 3 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Stage 1 — Base cases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1\}, 1) &= W[0][1] = 5 \\
C(\{2\}, 2) &= W[0][2] = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 2 — Subproblem $C(\{1, 2\}, \cdot)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(\{1, 2\}, 1) &= C(\{2\}, 2) + W[2][1] = 10 + 3 = 13 \\
C(\{1, 2\}, 2) &= C(\{1\}, 1) + W[1][2] = 5 + 3 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 3 — Close the tour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OPT} &= \min\{C(\{1,2\},1) + W[1][0],\; C(\{1,2\},2) + W[2][0]\} \\
&= \min\{13 + 5,\; 8 + 10\} \\
&= \min\{18,\; 18\} = \mathbf{18}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimal tour (via $i=1$):** $0 \to 2 \to 1 \to 0$ with cost $10 + 3 + 5 = 18$
**Optimal tour (via $i=2$):** $0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 0$ with cost $5 + 3 + 10 = 18$

Both tours are optimal with total cost **18**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

- ✅ **TSP** asks for the minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph.
- ✅ **Decision version** is **NP-Complete**; **optimization version** is **NP-Hard**.
- ✅ **Held–Karp DP** is the classical exact algorithm with complexity $O(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ — exponential but much better than $O(n!)$.
- ✅ **State:** $C(S, i)$ = min cost to traverse set $S$ ending at $i$, with $0 \notin S$.
- ✅ **Base case:** $C(\{i\}, i) = W[0][i]$.
- ✅ **Recurrence:** $C(S, i) = \min_{j \in S,\, j \neq i} \{C(S \setminus \{i\}, j) + W[j][i]\}$.
- ✅ **Final answer:** $\min_{i} \{C(\{1..n-1\}, i) + W[i][0]\}$.
- ✅ **Bitmask representation:** A subset of $\{1..n-1\}$ is encoded as an $n$-bit integer; bit $i$ set means city $i$ is in the subset.
- ✅ **Path reconstruction** requires storing the **arg-min** predecessor $j$ for every DP state.
- ✅ **Symmetric TSP:** $W[i][j] = W[j][i]$; only $(n-1)!/2$ distinct tours. **Asymmetric TSP:** $W[i][j] \neq W[j][i]$; $(n-1)!$ distinct tours.
- ✅ **Pseudo-polynomial** refers to time being polynomial in a numeric value ($2^{n}$), not in $\log$-scaled input size.
- ✅ **Practical limit:** Held–Karp can solve TSP exactly only up to $n \approx 20-25$ cities on modern hardware; for larger instances, use approximation algorithms (Nearest Neighbor, Christofides, Lin–Kernighan).
- ✅ **KTU lab test tip:** Always include the $W[i][0]$ return edge when computing the final answer; without it you lose **2 marks** instantly.
- ✅ **Common off-by-one:** City indices run $0$ to $n-1$, but bitmask bits for cities run $1$ to $n-1$ (city $0$ is never in $S$).
- ✅ **Memoization vs Tabulation:** Both work; the recursive memoized form is more intuitive and directly mirrors the recurrence relation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement Dynamic Programming Algorithm to Find the Solution to 0/1 Knapsack Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCITL508 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement Dynamic Programming Algorithm to Find the Solution to 0/1 Knapsack Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **0/1 Knapsack Problem (KP):** Given a set of $n$ items, where each item $i$ has an associated weight $w_i$ and value $v_i$, and a knapsack with a maximum weight capacity $W$, the goal is to select a subset of items such that the total weight does not exceed $W$ and the total value is **maximized**. The "0/1" constraint means each item can be either **taken as a whole (1) or not taken at all (0)** — fractional selection is not permitted.

The mathematical formulation is:

$$\text{Maximize} \quad Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot x_i$$

$$\text{Subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \leq W, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}$$

where $x_i = 1$ if item $i$ is selected, and $x_i = 0$ otherwise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hiking Backpack

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine you are a hiker preparing for a 3-day trek. Your backpack can carry at most **15 kg**. You have a list of survival items (tent, food, water, first-aid, flashlight, etc.), each with a **weight** and a **survival value/importance** to you. You cannot tear a tent in half (no fractions). You must pick the *combination* of items that gives you the **highest total value** without exceeding the 15 kg limit. That is precisely the 0/1 Knapsack Problem.

### 1.3 Why Dynamic Programming?

The brute-force approach tries all $2^n$ subsets — exponential time. Since the problem exhibits **Optimal Substructure** and **Overlapping Subproblems**, it is a textbook candidate for **Dynamic Programming (DP)**.

| Property | Meaning in 0/1 KP |
|---|---|
| **Optimal Substructure** | The optimal solution for capacity $W$ using $i$ items is built from optimal solutions of smaller subproblems. |
| **Overlapping Subproblems** | The same subproblem (e.g., "best value for capacity 5 using first 3 items") is solved many times. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used:**
> - **Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W)$
> - **Space Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W)$, reducible to $O(W)$ using 1-D array.
> - **Pseudo-polynomial:** Runtime depends on the magnitude of $W$ (not just $\log W$), making it pseudo-polynomial in input size.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The DP Recurrence Relation
